## BEIT:

## BERT PRE-TRAINING OF IMAGE TRANSFORMERS

**Hangbo Bao** _[†∗]_ **, Li Dong** _[‡]_ **, Songhao Piao** _[†]_ **, Furu Wei** _[‡]_

_†_ Harbin Institute of Technology

_‡_ Microsoft Research

https://github.com/microsoft/unilm

## ABSTRACT

We introduce a self-supervised vision representation model **BEIT** , which stands for **B** idirectional **E** ncoder representation from **I** mage **T** ransformers. Following BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) developed in the natural language processing area, we propose a _masked image modeling_ task to pretrain vision Transformers. Specifically, each image has two views in our pre-training, i.e., image patches (such as 16 _×_ 16 pixels), and visual tokens (i.e., discrete tokens). We first “tokenize” the original image into visual tokens. Then we randomly mask some image patches and fed them into the backbone Transformer. The pre-training objective is to recover the original visual tokens based on the corrupted image patches. After pre-training BEIT, we directly fine-tune the model parameters on downstream tasks by appending task layers upon the pretrained encoder. Experimental results on image classification and semantic segmentation show that our model achieves competitive results with previous pre-training methods.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) has achieved promising performance in computer vision (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020; Touvron et al., 2020). However, empirical studies show that vision Transformers require more training data than convolutional neural networks. In order to solve the data-hungry issue (Liu et al., 2021a), self-supervised pre-training is a promising solution to leverage large-scale image data. Several strands of methods have been explored for vision Transformers, such as contrastive learning (Chen et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2021), and self-distillation (Caron et al., 2021).

Concurrently, BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) has achieved great success in natural language processing. Its masked language modeling task first randomly masks some proportion of tokens within a text, and then recovers the masked tokens based on the Transformer encoding results of the corrupted text. Motivated by BERT, we turn to the denoising auto-encoding idea to pretrain vision Transformers, which has not been well studied by the vision community. It is challenging to directly apply BERTstyle pre-training for image data. First of all, there is no pre-exist vocabulary for vision Transformer’s input unit, i.e., image patches. So we cannot simply employ a softmax classifier to predict over all possible candidates for masked patches. In contrast, the language vocabulary, such as words and BPE (Sennrich et al., 2016), is well-defined and eases auto-encoding prediction. A straightforward alternative is regarding the task as a regression problem, which predicts the raw pixels of masked patches. However, such pixel-level recovery task tends to waste modeling capability on pre-training short-range dependencies and high-frequency details (Ramesh et al., 2021). Our goal is to overcome the above issues for pre-training of vision Transformers.

In this work, we introduce a self-supervised vision representation model **BEIT** , which stands for **B** idirectional **E** ncoder representation from **I** mage **T** ransformers. Inspired by BERT, we propose a pre-training task, namely, masked image modeling (MIM). As shown in Figure 1, MIM uses two views for each images, i.e., image patches, and visual tokens. We split the image into a grid of patches that are the input representation of backbone Transformer. Moreover, we “tokenize” the image to discrete visual tokens, which is obtained by the latent codes of discrete VAE (Ramesh et al., 2021).

Figure 1: Overview of BEIT pre-training. Before pre-training, we learn an “image tokenizer” via autoencoding-style reconstruction, where an image is tokenized into discrete visual tokens according to the learned vocabulary. During pre-training, each image has two views, i.e., image patches, and visual tokens. We randomly mask some proportion of image patches (gray patches in the figure) and replace them with a special mask embedding [M]. Then the patches are fed to a backbone vision Transformer. The pre-training task aims at predicting the visual tokens of the _original_ image based on the encoding vectors of the _corrupted_ image.

During pre-training, we randomly mask some proportion of image patches, and feed the corrupted input to Transformer. The model learns to recover the visual tokens of the original image, instead of the raw pixels of masked patches.

We perform self-supervised learning and then fine-tune the pretrained BEIT on two downstream tasks, i.e., image classification, and semantic segmentation. Experimental results indicate that BEIT outperforms both from-scratch training and previous strong self-supervised models. Moreover, BEIT is complementary to supervised pre-training. Performance of BEIT can be further improved by intermediate fine-tuning with ImageNet labels. Ablation studies show that our proposed techniques are critical to the effectiveness of BERT-style pre-training for image data. Apart from performance, the improvements of convergence speed and stability of fine-tuning reduce training costs on end tasks. In addition, we demonstrate that self-supervised BEIT can learn reasonable semantic regions via pre-training, unleashing the rich supervision signals contained in images.

Our contributions are summarized as follows:

- We propose a masked image modeling task to pretrain vision Transformers in a self-supervised manner. We also provide a theoretical explanation from the perspective of variational autoencoder.
- We pretrain BEIT and conduct extensive fine-tuning experiments on downstream tasks, such as image classification, and semantic segmentation.
- We present that the self-attention mechanism of self-supervised BEIT learns to distinguish semantic regions and object boundaries, although without using any human annotation.

## 2 METHODS

Given an input image _x_ , BEIT encodes it to contextualized vector representations. As shown in Figure 1, BEIT is pretrained by the masked image modeling (MIM) task in a self-supervised learning manner. MIM aims at recovering the masked image patches based on encoding vectors. For

downstream tasks (such as image classification, and semantic segmentation), we append task layers upon pretrained BEIT and fine-tune the parameters on the specific datasets.

## 2.1 IMAGE REPRESENTATIONS

The images have two views of representations in our method, namely, _image patch_ , and _visual tokens_ . The two types serve as input and output representations during pre-training, respectively.

## 2.1.1 IMAGE PATCH

The 2D image is split into a sequence of patches (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020), so that a standard Transformer can directly accept image data. Formally, we reshape the image _**x** ∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ into _N_ = _HW/P_[2] patches _**x**[p] ∈_ R _[N][×]_[(] _[P]_[ 2] _[C]_[)] , where _C_ is the number of channels, ( _H, W_ ) is the input image resolution, and ( _P, P_ ) is the resolution of each patch. The image patches _{_ _**x**[p] i[}] i[N]_ =1[are flattened] into vectors and are linearly projected, which is similar to word embeddings in BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Image patches preserve raw pixels and are used as input features in BEIT.
In our experiments, we split each 224 _×_ 224 image into a 14 _×_ 14 grid of image patches, where each patch is 16 _×_ 16.

## 2.1.2 VISUAL TOKEN

Similar to natural language, we represent the image as a sequence of discrete tokens obtained by an “image tokenizer”, instead of raw pixels. Specifically, we tokenize the image _**x** ∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ into _**z**_ = [ _z_ 1 _, . . . , zN_ ] _∈V[h][×][w]_ , where the vocabulary _V_ = _{_ 1 _, . . . , |V|}_ contains discrete token indices.

Following (Ramesh et al., 2021), we use the image tokenizer learned by discrete variational autoencoder (dVAE). There are two modules during visual token learning, namely, _tokenizer_ and _decoder_ . The tokenizer _qφ_ ( _**z** |_ _**x**_ ) maps image pixels _**x**_ into discrete tokens _**z**_ according to a visual codebook (i.e., vocabulary). The decoder _pψ_ ( _**x** |_ _**z**_ ) learns to reconstruct the input image _**x**_ based on the visual tokens _**z**_ . The reconstruction objective can be written as E _**z** ∼qφ_ ( **z** _|_ _**x**_ )[log _pψ_ ( _**x** |_ _**z**_ )]. Because the latent visual tokens are discrete, the model training is non-differentiable. Gumbel-softmax relaxation (Jang et al., 2017; Maddison et al., 2017) is employed to train the model parameters. Moreover, a uniform prior is put on _qφ_ during dVAE training. Refer to (Ramesh et al., 2021) for more training details of the image tokenizer.

We tokenize each image to a 14 _×_ 14 grid of visual tokens. Notice the number of visual tokens and the number of image patches for one image are the same. The vocabulary size is set to _|V|_ = 8192. In our work, we directly use the publicly available[1] image tokenizer described in (Ramesh et al., 2021). We also compare it with a re-implemented tokenizer in Appendix C.

## 2.3 PRE-TRAINING BEIT: MASKED IMAGE MODELING
We propose a _masked image modeling_ (MIM) task. We randomly mask some percentage of image patches, and then predict the visual tokens that are corresponding to the masked patches.

The MIM task is greatly inspired by masked language modeling (Devlin et al., 2019), which is one of the most successful pre-training objective in natural language processing. Moreover, blockwise (or n-gram) masking is also widely applied in BERT-like models (Joshi et al., 2020; Bao et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020). However, directly using pixel-level auto-encoding (i.e., recovering the pixels of masked patches) for vision pre-training pushes the model to focus on short-range dependencies and high-frequency details (Ramesh et al., 2021). BEIT overcomes the above issue by predicting discrete visual tokens, which summarizes the details to high-level abstractions. Ablation studies in Section 3.3 show that our proposed method significantly outperforms pixel-level auto-encoding.

## 2.5 PRE-TRAINING SETUP

The network architecture of BEIT follows that of ViT-Base (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) for a fair comparison. We use a 12-layer Transformer with 768 hidden size, and 12 attention heads. The intermediate size of feed-forward networks is 3072. We employ the default 16 _×_ 16 input patch size. We directly borrow the image tokenizer trained by Ramesh et al. (2021). The vocabulary size of visual tokens is 8192.

We pretrain BEIT on the training set of ImageNet-1K (Russakovsky et al., 2015), which contains about 1 _._ 2M images. Our augmentation policy includes random resized cropping, horizontal flipping, color jittering (Wu et al., 2018). Notice that we do not use the labels for self-supervised learning. We use the 224 _×_ 224 resolution in our experiments. So the input is split to 14 _×_ 14 image patches, and the same amount of visual tokens. We randomly mask at most 75 patches (i.e., roughly 40% of total image patches).

The pre-training runs for about 500k steps (i.e., 800 epochs) with 2k batch size. Adam (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019) with _β_ 1 = 0 _._ 9 _, β_ 2 = 0 _._ 999 is employed for optimization. The learning rate is set to 1.5e-3, with a warmup of 10 epochs, and cosine learning rate decay. The weight decay is 0 _._ 05. We employ stochastic depth (Huang et al., 2016) with a 0 _._ 1 rate, and disable dropout. The 500k training steps take about five days using 16 Nvidia Telsa V100 32GB GPU cards.

We find that proper initialization is important to stabilize Transformer, especially for large-scale pretraining. We first randomly initialize all the parameters within a small range, such as [ _−_ 0 _._ 02 _,_ 0 _._ 02]. Then, for the _l_ -th Transformer layer, we rescale the output matrices (i.e., the last linear projection within each sub-layer) of the self-attention module and the feed-forward network by ~~_√_~~ 12 _l_[.]

## 2.6 FINE-TUNING BEIT ON DOWNSTREAM VISION TASKS

After pre-training BEIT, we append a task layer upon the Transformer, and fine-tune the parameters on downstream tasks, like BERT. We take image classification and semantic segmentation as examples in our work. It is straightforward to leverage the pre-training-then-fine-tuning paradigm on other vision tasks with BEIT.

**Image classification.** For image classification tasks, we directly employ a simple linear classifier as the task layer. Specifically, we use average pooling to aggregate the representations, and feed the global to a softmax classifier. The category probabilities are computed as softmax(avg( _{_ _**h**[L] i[}][N] i_ =1 _**[W]**[c]_[))][,][where] _**[h]**[L] i_[is][the][final][encoding][vector][of][the] _[i]_[-th][image][patch,] _**W** c ∈_ R _[D][×][C]_ is a parameter matrix, and _C_ is the number of labels. We maximize the likelihood of labeled data by updating the parameters of BEIT and the softmax classifier.

**Semantic segmentation.** For semantic segmentation, we follow the task layer used in SETRPUP (Zheng et al., 2020). To be specific, we use pretrained BEIT as a backbone encoder, and incorporate several deconvolution layers as decoder to produce segmentation. The model is also end-to-end fine-tuned similar to image classification.

**Intermediate fine-tuning.** After self-supervised pre-training, we can further train BEIT on a datarich intermediate dataset (i.e., ImageNet-1K in our work), and then finetune the model on the target downstream tasks. Such intermediate fine-tuning is the common practice of BERT fine-tuning in NLP (Pruksachatkun et al., 2020). We directly follow the method for BEIT.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

We conduct full fine-tuning experiments on image classification and semantic segmentation. Moreover, we present various ablation studies for pre-training and analyze the representations learned by BEIT. We also report linear probes on ImageNet in Appendix D.

## 3.1 IMAGE CLASSIFICATION

The image classification task classifies input images to various categories. We evaluate BEIT on the ILSVRC-2012 ImageNet dataset (Russakovsky et al., 2015) with 1k classes and 1 _._ 3M images. We directly follow the most of hyperparameters of DeiT (Touvron et al., 2020) in our fine-tuning experiments for a fair comparison. We reduce fine-tuning epochs compared with training from scratch, as BEIT has been pre-trained. Accordingly, we use a larger learning rate with layer-wise decay. The detailed hyperparameters are summarized in Appendix H.

Table 1 reports top-1 accuracy on image classification. We compare BEIT with vision Transformers trained by random initialization, supervised pre-training, and previous self-supervised learning methods. All the compared models are base-size, except iGPT has 1.36B parameters. Pre-training is conducted on ImageNet for the comparison purpose, except ViT-JFT300M is pretrained on Google’s in-house 300M images.

Compared with the models trained by random initialization, we find that pre-trained BEIT significantly improves performance on both datasets. BEIT improves the performance on ImageNet, which shows the effectiveness under the rich-resource setting.

Moreover, we compare BEIT with previous state-of-the-art self-supervised methods for Transformer, such as DINO (Caron et al., 2021), and MoCo v3 (Chen et al., 2021). Our proposed method outperforms previous models on ImageNet fine-tuning. Among them, iGPT-1.36B (Chen et al., 2020a) uses much more parameters (i.e., 1 _._ 36B vs 86M), and ViT-JFT300M (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) is pretrained on larger corpus (i.e., 300M vs 1 _._ 3M), while others pretrain ViT-Base on ImageNet-1K. iGPT-1.36B and ViT-JFT300M are the most comparable methods, which also follows auto-encoding pre-training for vision Transformer. Specifically, iGPT uses clustered image tokens as both input and output for image GPT or image BERT. In contrast, we use image patches as input to preserve raw pixels, and employ discrete visual tokens as a prediction bottleneck. ViT-JFT300 predicts the mean, 3-bit color of each masked patch, rather than visual tokens learned by discrete VAE. We also pretrain the self-supervised tasks of BEIT and DINO in a multi-task learning manner, which is presented in Appendix E.

In addition, we evaluate our proposed method with intermediate fine-tuning. In other words, we first pretrain BEIT in a self-supervised manner, and then fine-tune the pretrained model on ImageNet with labeled data. The results show that BEIT is complementary to supervised pre-training, achieving additional gain after intermediate fine-tuning on ImageNet.

**Fine-tuning to** 384 _×_ 384 **resolution.** After fine-tuning with resolution 224 _×_ 224, we additionally fine-tune the model on 384 _×_ 384 images by 10 more epochs. We follow the standard higher-resolution setting of DeiT (Touvron et al., 2020), except using fewer epochs. Notice that we keep patch size the same for both 224 _×_ 224 and 384 _×_ 384 images. So the input sequence length of Transformers becomes longer for higher resolutions. Table 1 shows that higher resolution improves the BEIT results by 1+ points on ImageNet. More importantly, BEIT384 pretrained on ImageNet-1K even outperforms supervised pre-training ViT384 that uses ImageNet-22K, when they use the same input resolution.

**Scaling up to larger size.** We further scale up BEIT to the large size (same as ViT-L). As shown in Table 1, ViT384-L is worse than ViT384 on ImageNet, when training from scratch. The results verifies the data-hungry issue of vision Transformers. Supervised pre-training on ImageNet-22K partially relieves the issue, where ViT384-L finally outperforms ViT384 by 1 _._ 2. In comparison, BEIT-L is better than BEIT by 2 _._ 0, and BEIT384-L outperforms BEIT384 by 1 _._ 7. In other words, the benefits of scaling up BEIT from base to large are greater than supervised pre-training with ImageNet-22K. More importantly, comparing between BEIT384 with ViT384 that conducts supervised pre-training on ImageNet-22K, the improvements of BEIT become greater along with scaling the size from base

## 3.2 SEMANTIC SEGMENTATION

Semantic segmentation aims to predict a corresponding class for each pixel of the input image. We evaluate BEIT on the ADE20K benchmark (Zhou et al., 2019) with 25K images and 150 semantic categories. We report the metric of mean Intersection of Union (mIoU) averaged over all semantic categories. As presented in Section 2.6, we directly follow the task layer and the most of hyperparameters described in SETR-PUP (Zheng et al., 2020). On ADE20K, we use Adam (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019) as the optimizer. The learning rate is set to 1e-3 with layer-wise decay similar to image classification. We conduct fine-tuning for 160K steps. The batch size is 16. The detailed hyperparameters are described in Appendix I.

As shown in Table 3, we compare BEIT with supervised pre-training that relies on labeled data of ImageNet. We find that our proposed method achieves better performance than supervised pretraining, although BEIT does not require manual annotations for pre-training. Moreover, we employ intermediate fine-tuning for BEIT on ImageNet, i.e., we first fine-tune pretrained BEIT on ImageNet, and then fine-tune the model on ADE20K. The results indicate that intermediate fine-tuning further improves BEIT on semantic segmentation.

## 3.3 ABLATION STUDIES

We conduct ablation studies to analyze the contributions of each component in BEIT. The models are evaluated on image classification (i.e., ImageNet) and semantic segmentation (i.e., ADE20K). We set the default pre-training steps to 300 epochs for the ablation studies, which is 37 _._ 5% of the total steps used in the previous experiments.

Table 4 reports the results of various model variants. First, we ablate blockwise masking by randomly sample masked positions. We find that blockwise masking is beneficial on both tasks, especially on semantic segmentation. Second, we ablate the usage of visual tokens by predicting the raw pixels of masked patches, i.e., the pre-training task becomes a pixel regression problem to recover masked patches. Our proposed masked image modeling task significantly outperforms naive pixel-level auto-encoding. Compared with the results in Table 1, the ablation result is worse than training vision Transformer from scratch on two tasks. The results indicate that the prediction of visual tokens is the key ingredient of BEIT. Third, we ablate the usage of visual tokens and blockwise masking together. We find that blockwise masking is even more helpful for pixel-level auto-encoding, which relieves the suffering of short-distance dependency. Forth, recovering all the visual tokens harms performance on downstream tasks. Fifth, we compare BEIT with different training steps. Pre-training the model longer can further improve performance on downstream tasks.

## 3.4 ANALYSIS OF SELF-ATTENTION MAP

We show that the self-attention mechanism in BEIT can separate objects, even though our pre-training does not rely on any manual annotation at all. Similar properties are also observed by Caron et al. (2021). The probing images are taken from the MS COCO (Lin et al., 2014) corpus to avoid appearing in the pre-training data.

Figure 2: Self-attention map for different reference points. The self-attention mechanism in BEIT is able to separate objects, although self-supervised pre-training does not use manual annotations.

As shown in Figure 2, we plot the self-attention map for different reference points within an image. The visualizations are produced by attention scores computed via query-key product in the last layer. For each reference point, we use the corresponding patch as query, and show which patch it attends to. After pre-training, BEIT learns to distinguish semantic regions using self-attention heads, without any task-specific supervision. The property partially indicates the reason why BEIT is able to help downstream tasks. Such knowledge acquired by BEIT potentially improves the generalization ability of fine-tuned models, especially on small-scale datasets.

## 4 RELATED WORK

**Self-supervised visual representation learning.** Various methods have been introduced over the years to pretrain vision models in a self-supervised manner. Pioneering works design clever pretext tasks, such as predicting the patch orderings (Noroozi & Favaro, 2016), colorization (Zhang et al., 2016), and predicting rotation angles (Komodakis & Gidaris, 2018). In addition, Trinh et al. (2019) propose to mask some patches within an image, and classify whether the masked patches are real or fake for each masked position. The method is similar to the masked version of Jigsaw pretraining (Noroozi & Favaro, 2016). The recent strand of research follows contrastive paradigm (Wu et al., 2018; Oord et al., 2018; Hjelm et al., 2019; Bachman et al., 2019; He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020b;c). The models typically regard various data augmentations as different views of an image, and then make the representations of positive pairs similar while pushing negative pairs away. In order to obtain enough informative negative samples in contrastive learning, the methods usually rely on large memory banks (Wu et al., 2018; He et al., 2020) or large batch size (Chen et al., 2020b). BYOL (Grill et al., 2020) and SimSiam (Chen & He, 2020) further eliminate the requirement of negative samples, using various techniques to avoid representation collapse. Another strand of methods use clustering to organize image examples (Caron et al., 2018; Asano et al., 2020; Caron et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021).

**Self-supervised vision Transformers.** Pre-training vision Transformers has received significant attention recently due to the data-hungry issue. iGPT (Chen et al., 2020a) first creates a 9-bit color palette by k-means clustering RGB pixels, and then uses the clustered tokens to represent images. Next iGPT uses the tasks of BERT and GPT to pretrain Transformers. In comparison, our proposed method uses image patches as input without losing pixel-level information. Moreover, our visual tokens are obtained by discrete VAE instead of clustering. ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) conducts a preliminary exploration with the masked patch prediction task, which predicts the 3-bit mean color of the masked patches. Dosovitskiy et al. (2020) also report that pixel-level auto-encoding performs

worse, although it is the most straightforward translation of BERT from NLP to CV. Rather than using heuristically designed pre-training tasks, our proposed model leverages visual tokens learned by discrete VAE, which not only achieves better performance but also is better theoretically motivated. Apart from masked auto-encoding, other mainstream research works use contrastive learning (Chen et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2021), and self-distillation (Caron et al., 2021). In comparison, BEIT can achieve several times of improvement in terms of pre-training throughput (Appendix E), and memory consumption. The advantages make BEIT appealing to scale up vision Transformers.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We introduce a self-supervised pre-training framework for vision Transformers, achieving strong fine-tuning results on downstream tasks, such as image classification, and semantic segmentation. We show that the proposed method is critical to make BERT-like pre-training (i.e., auto-encoding with masked input) work well for image Transformers. We also present the intriguing property of automatically acquired knowledge about semantic regions, without using any human-annotated data. In the future, we would like to scale up BEIT pre-training in terms of data size and model size. Moreover, we will conduct multimodal pre-training in a more unified way, using the similar objectives and the shared architecture for texts and images.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a significant and well-executed adaptation of the BERT paradigm to the computer vision domain. The quality of the work is high, characterized by a clear motivation to overcome the "data-hungry" nature of Vision Transformers (ViTs) through self-supervised pre-training. The authors provide a compelling technical bridge between discrete language tokens and continuous image data by utilizing an image tokenizer (dVAE) to facilitate a "Masked Image Modeling" (MIM) task. The clarity of the writing and the accompanying architectural diagrams make the methodology easy to follow, and the theoretical grounding—linking the approach to variational autoencoders—adds substantial academic rigor.

The most significant issue, however, lies in the complexity of the two-stage training pipeline. Unlike generative approaches (like MAE) or contrastive approaches (like DINO) that operate directly on pixels or features, BEIT requires a pre-trained discrete VAE to generate the "vocabulary" for its objective. This introduces a heavy dependency on the tokenizer's quality and increases the computational overhead. Furthermore, while the paper highlights superior downstream performance, it could more explicitly address the trade-off between the additional complexity of the tokenization stage versus the performance gains. Overall, this is a seminal contribution that effectively pioneers denoising auto-encoding for ViTs, though its reliance on an external tokenizer remains a notable bottleneck in terms of pipeline simplicity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'two views' terminology
comments.quote: Specifically, each image has two views in our pre-training, i.e., image patches (such as 16 × 16 pixels), and visual tokens (i.e., discrete tokens).
comments.explanation: In self-supervised learning for vision (e.g., SimCLR, BYOL, DINO), 'two views' almost universally refers to two different augmentations/crops of the same image used as inputs to the model. Here, the authors use 'two views' to describe the 'input representation' (patches) and the 'target representation' (visual tokens). This risks significant confusion regarding the architecture's data flow for readers familiar with contrastive learning benchmarks.

If x is reshaped into N patches, the notation R^[N] x [(] [P]^2 [C] [)] implies a single matrix containing all patches, but then defines the patch variable x_p as having N as its first dimension, effectively stating that each individual patch contains N sub-elements. A more precise formulation would be x_p \in R^{P^2 C} for each index i=1...N.

comments.title: Tense inconsistency regarding masking and model input
comments.quote: Then we randomly mask some image patches and fed them into the backbone Transformer.
comments.explanation: In the Abstract, the authors explain the Masked Image Modeling task. The sentence structure suggests that the masked patches themselves are 'fed' into the Transformer. However, the standard Transformer cannot process a 'masked patch' (which is effectively null or a gap) without a numerical representation. While most readers would assume a mask embedding is used, the phrasing 'fed them' is slightly ambiguous in this summary. Upon checking Section 2.3, the authors clarify that masked patches are replaced with a learnable embedding [M]. While common in the field, the abstract's phrasing 'fed them [masked patches]' could be interpreted as feeding the original pixels of patches that are marked for masking, which would be an information leak. Given this is the abstract and Section 2.3 clarifies the procedure, this is a minor clarity issue rather than a technical error.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'two views' terminology
comments.quote: Specifically, each image has two views in our pre-training, i.e., image patches (such as 16 × 16 pixels), and visual tokens (i.e., discrete tokens).
comments.explanation: In self-supervised learning for vision (e.g., SimCLR, BYOL, DINO), 'two views' almost universally refers to two different augmentations/crops of the same image used as inputs to the model. Here, the authors use 'two views' to describe the 'input representation' (patches) and the 'target representation' (visual tokens). While Section 2.1 clearly defines these, the use of 'views' in the abstract and Introduction might mislead a reader familiar with contrastive learning into thinking the model utilizes Siamese-style multi-view inputs. This is a framing choice rather than a technical error, but it risks initial confusion regarding the architecture's data flow.

If x is reshaped into N patches, each individual patch should have the dimension R^{P^2 C}. The notation R^[N] x [(] [P]^2 [C] [)] implies a single matrix containing all patches. While the intent is clear (splitting an image into a grid), the formal definition presents N as the total count of patches but then defines the patch variable x_p as having N as its first dimension, effectively stating that each patch contains N sub-elements of size P^2C. A more precise formulation would be x_p \in R^{P^2 C} for each index i=1...N. This is a common point of confusion in ViT papers, but here the dimensions in the bracket R[N]... conflict with the definition of i=1 to N used later.

comments.explanation: I initially suspected an OCR error for the sampling range of the aspect ratio 'r'. However, looking at the full context of Algorithm 1, the range is defined as 'Rand(0.3, 0.3)'. In stochastic block masking (as seen in BEiT's inspirations like SpanBERT or SimMIM), the aspect ratio is meant to vary to create diverse mask shapes. Sampling from a range where the minimum and maximum are both 0.3 renders the 'Rand' function a constant, which contradicts the prose in Section 2.3 stating 'we randomly choose an aspect ratio for the masking block'. comments.quote: In the second stage, we learn the priorwith the most likely visual tokens $p_\theta$ while keeping $z$ ˆ $q_i \phi$ = arg max and $p_\psi$ fixed. $z q_\phi$ We simplify( $z|x_i$ ).
comments.explanation: The OCR has significantly mangled this derivation, but there is a logic error beyond the transcription. The text states they learn the 'prior' $p_\theta$ while keeping the encoder $q_\phi$ and decoder $p_\psi$ fixed. However, in the context of Equation (2) and the MIM objective, the backbone Transformer *is* the parameterized model being trained to approximate the conditional distribution of tokens. Scaling *up* by $\sqrt{12l}$ (if that is what is intended) would likely lead to divergence. If the intended notation is $1/\sqrt{2l}$ or $1/\sqrt{L}$, it is a common technique, but as written, the formula is ambiguous and potentially mathematically inverse to what is required for stability.

comments.quote: In comparison, BEIT-L is better than BEIT by 2 . 0, and BEIT384-L outperforms BEIT384 by 1 . 7. In other words, the benefits of scaling up BEIT from base to large are greater than supervised pre-training with ImageNet-22K.

Looking at the logic: they observe that ViT-L (supervised) is 1.2 points better than ViT-Base (supervised), while BEIT-L is 2.0 points better than BEIT-Base. While the delta is larger, the claim implies that BEIT handles the 'data-hungry' nature better than supervised learning. However, the supervised comparisons are often confounded by the specific regularization and augmentation used (DeiT vs original ViT). This indicates that the aspect ratio is fixed to exactly 0.3 for every block, which contradicts the prose earlier in Section 2.3 stating 'we randomly choose an aspect ratio for the masking block.' Given the context of the paper (mimicking NLP n-grams and varying block shapes), a search through common implementations of BEIT suggests the intended range was likely `(0.3, 1/0.3)` or similar. comments.title: Ambiguous Loss Normalization in ELBO Derivation
comments.quote: $\log p(x|\tilde{x}) \geq \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{z \sim q_{\phi}(z|x)} [\log p_{\psi}(x|z)]}_{\text{Visual Token Reconstruction}} - D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(z|x) || p_{\theta}(z|\tilde{x}))$
comments.explanation: In Section 2.4, the authors provide a VAE-based theoretical framework. Equation (2) defines the first term as 'Visual Token Reconstruction'. However, the formula $\mathbb{E}_{z \sim q_{\phi}(z|x)} [\log p_{\psi}(x|z)]$ represents the reconstruction of image pixels $x$ from latent tokens $z$ (the dVAE's job), not the reconstruction of the tokens themselves. The second term $D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(z|x) || p_{\theta}(z|\tilde{x}))$ is what actually relates to the MIM task (predicting $z$). In the subsequent simplification for Stage 2, the authors claim the BEIT objective is the 'second term', but the notation in Equation (3) labels the simplified likelihood as $\mathbb{E}_{z_i \sim q_{\phi}(z|x_i)} [\log p_{\psi}(x_i|z_i)]$, which is the pixel reconstruction loss. There is a conceptual swap between the 'prior' $p_{\theta}(z|\tilde{x})$ and the 'reconstruction' $p_{\psi}(x|z)$ in the prose vs the equations. A reader trying to map the MIM objective directly to the math might be confused whether they are maximizing the probability of tokens or pixels according to the ELBO labels.

comments.quote: we rescale the output matrices [...] of the self-attention module and the feed-forward network by $\sqrt{12/l}$.
comments.explanation: The summary provided in the context (and standard DeepNet/foundation model initialization practices) suggests the rescaling factor should be $\sqrt{l/L}$ or $\sqrt{1/l}$ to stabilize deep layers. The text in Section 2.5 says $\sqrt{12/l}$. With $L=12$, at layer $l=12$, the multiplier is $\sqrt{1}=1$. However, at layer $l=1$, the multiplier is $\sqrt{12} \approx 3.46$. It is highly unusual in Transformer literature to *increase* the variance of early layers by a factor of 3.5 to stabilize training; usually, late layers are suppressed or all layers are scaled down. If this is intended to be $1/\sqrt{2l}$ or $\sqrt{1/2l}$ (as seen in some implementations), the OCR or the transcription $\sqrt{12/l}$ appears mathematically suspicious as a stabilization technique.

comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the variable $w$ is defined in the input as the number of patches in the width dimension ($N = h \times w$). The algorithm calculates the block height $a$ and width $b$. To ensure the indices are within bounds, the horizontal starting position $l$ should be sampled from $[0, w - b]$. However, the OCR text and the logic suggest a potential boundary error if the range is inclusive. More critically, the text $r \leftarrow \text{Rand}(0.3, 0.3)$ implies a fixed aspect ratio of 1:1, whereas the prose describes 'randomly choosing an aspect ratio'. While the fixed values 0.3 might be an OCR error for a range like (0.3, 3.0), as written, it contradicts the claim of random aspect ratio selection. Furthermore, the math for $a$ and $b$ using $s$ (total patches) and $r$ (aspect ratio) usually takes the form $a = \sqrt{s \cdot r}$ and $b = \sqrt{s/r}$ (or vice versa), but the OCR symbols (~~[\sqrt]~~ and ~~~~) make the exact operation ambiguous, though the intent is recoverable.

comments.title: Simplification of dVAE distribution to one-point distribution
comments.quote: We simplify $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}|x_i)$ to a one-point distribution $z_i = \arg \max_z q_{\phi}(z|x_i)$. Then Equation (2) can be rewritten as: ... where the second term is our BEIT pre-training objective.
comments.explanation: In Section 2.4, the authors justify the MIM task as optimizing the ELBO of a VAE. They state that by simplifying the encoder $q_\phi$ to a delta function (the argmax), the KL divergence term $D_{KL}(q_\phi(z|x) || p_\theta(z|\tilde{x}))$ in Equation 2 reduces to the cross-entropy loss used in MIM. While this is a common pedagogical simplification in latent variable models, it ignores the fact that $p_\theta$ in Equation 2 is conditioned on the *corrupted* image $\tilde{x}$, whereas the 'ground truth' $z_i$ comes from the *original* image $x$. The move from Equation 2 to Equation 3 is non-trivial because the KL divergence usually compares distributions over the same space. If $q$ is a delta function at $z_i$, then $D_{KL}(\delta_{z_i} || p_\theta)$ is simply $-\log p_\theta(z_i | \tilde{x})$. This mathematically aligns with the MIM objective, but the transition omits that the first term in Equation 2 (the reconstruction $p_\psi(x|z)$) is discarded because the dVAE is kept frozen. This is a logical bridge that is slightly underspecified for a reader trying to follow the ELBO derivation strictly.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in initialization scaling
comments.quote: rescale the output matrices ... by $\sqrt{1 / 2l}$
comments.explanation: In Section 2.5, the text describes a stabilization technique for the Transformer: 'rescale the output matrices... by $\sqrt{1 / 2l}$'. Looking at the notation summary provided earlier, which states $\sqrt{l/12}$, and standard practice in papers like DeepNet or Fixup initialization, this formula appears to be an OCR misread or a typo. Usually, one scales by a factor related to the depth $L$ or $l$. If the intended value was $1/\sqrt{2l}$ or a factor involving the total layers 12, the current expression '$\sqrt{1/2l}$' (square root of one over two-l) versus the summary's '$\sqrt{l/12}$' is contradictory. Given that $l$ is the layer index, $\sqrt{l/12}$ would increase weights with depth while $1/\sqrt{2l}$ would decrease them. This ambiguity makes the stabilization method difficult to replicate precisely.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents BEIT, a novel self-supervised pre-training framework for vision Transformers inspired by BERT’s masked language modeling, and makes a strong conceptual contribution by bridging ideas from NLP to vision through the introduction of masked image modeling (MIM). The overall quality of the paper is high, with a clear motivation, well-structured methodology, and compelling experimental validation. The clarity of exposition is generally strong—especially in the introduction and method sections—where the core idea of using discrete visual tokens as targets for reconstruction is clearly motivated and situated within the context of prior work. However, a significant conceptual issue lies in the early presentation: the paper refers to "visual tokens" and an "image tokenizer" without immediately defining their source, which creates momentary confusion until the role of the discrete VAE (inspired by DALL·E) is introduced later. This could be mitigated with earlier clarification. Additionally, while the analogy to BERT is intuitive and helps frame the approach, the paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion of how masked patch prediction differs fundamentally from masked language modeling—particularly regarding the lack of an inherent vocabulary in vision and the reliance on a learned tokenizer. Despite these minor issues, the technical rigor, empirical breadth, and thoughtful ablation studies lend strong support to the claims. The proposed method achieves competitive performance on downstream tasks, and the insights into attention patterns provide valuable interpretability. Overall, the paper is well-executed and makes a meaningful advance in the field of self-supervised vision representation learning.

comments.title: Mismatch between prose and objective in masked image modeling description
comments.quote: MIM aims at recovering the masked image patches based on encoding vectors.
comments.explanation: The sentence suggests the model aims to recover raw pixels of masked patches, which contradicts the paper's actual method: BEIT predicts discrete visual tokens, not pixel values. The abstract and Section 2.3 clarify that the objective is to recover visual token IDs using a softmax classifier. This early definition misleads readers unfamiliar with the full method, potentially suggesting pixel regression until later clarification. Although the broader context resolves the correct objective, this initial discrepancy creates unnecessary confusion and should be corrected to align with the token prediction framework.

comments.title: Incorrect and underspecified blockwise masking in Algorithm 1 due to OCR corruption
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1, central to BEIT's method, contains severe OCR artifacts that render key parts unusable. First, 'Rand(0.3, 0.3)' implies deterministic sampling (fixed aspect ratio of 0.3), contradicting the stated goal of randomly choosing aspect ratios; standard implementations use a range like [0.3, 3.0]. Second, the block dimensions 'a' and 'b' contain corrupted symbols (e.g., ~~_[√]_~~), likely intended to be sqrt(s * r) and sqrt(s / r) or similar derivations from area and aspect ratio. Without clear, reproducible expressions, the algorithm cannot be implemented as written. This undermines the paper's methodological contribution and affects reproducibility. While intent can be inferred from context and prior work, the presentation fails to specify the actual sampling and dimension logic.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about recoverability of original image from ELBO derivation
comments.quote: Considering the evidence lower bound (ELBO) of the log-likelihood _p_ ( _x|x_ ˜), i.e., recovering the original image from its corrupted version:
comments.explanation: The paper frames the ELBO as a lower bound on _p(x|x̃)_, implying BEIT recovers the full original image. However, BEIT only predicts visual tokens for masked regions—discrete, bottlenecked representations—using a fixed dVAE decoder. It does not optimize a full pixel-space likelihood. The term _pψ(x|z)_ refers to the frozen dVAE decoder and is not trained during MIM pre-training. Thus, the ELBO derivation overreaches by suggesting the model estimates a full image density. A correct interpretation links MIM to conditional token prediction under a pretrained prior, not image recovery. This mischaracterization weakens the theoretical framing, especially in Section 2.4.

comments.title: Incoherent VAE section due to OCR corruption and missing equations
comments.quote: where (1)original image given input visual tokens; (3) _qφ_ ( _z|x_ ) denotes the image tokenizer that obtains visual tokens; (2) _pθ_ ( _z|x_ ˜) recovers the visual tokens based on the masked _pψ_ ( _x|z_ ) decodes the image, which is our MIM pre-training task.
comments.explanation: The VAE derivation in Section 2.4 suffers from multiple issues. First, the sentence structure is garbled due to likely OCR or copy-paste errors, making it impossible to parse the intended definitions of ELBO components: pψ(x|z), pθ(z|x̃), and qφ(z|x) are conflated syntactically. Second, a critical equation following 'Then Equation (2) can be rewritten as:' is entirely missing, breaking the logical flow of the derivation. Third, the one-point approximation qφ(z|x) ≈ δ(z − argmax qφ(z|x)) is introduced without justification, despite being a strong assumption that invalidates the variational lower bound unless carefully handled. Together, these issues severely undermine the paper's attempt to provide a theoretical foundation for MIM via the ELBO, rendering Section 2.4 unintelligible and incomplete.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for visual token space due to OCR artifacts
comments.quote: Specifically, we tokenize the image _**x** ∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ into _**z**_ = [ _z_ 1 _, . . . , zN_ ] _∈V[h][×][w]_
comments.explanation: The expression _z ∈ V[h][×][w]_ is syntactically invalid: V is defined as a discrete set of token indices {1, ..., |V|}, not a space, and [h][×][w] appears to be OCR corruption of 'h×w'. The correct notation should be z ∈ {1, ..., |V|}^{h × w} or [V]^{h × w}. While the surrounding text clarifies that z consists of N discrete tokens corresponding to patches (h = w = 14), the malformed notation introduces ambiguity, especially for readers attempting formal interpretation. This is likely a typesetting issue, but it still undermines precision in a foundational definition.

comments.title: Missing pre-training objective equation
comments.quote:
The pre-training objective is to maximize the log-likelihood of the correct visual tokens _zi_ given the corrupted image:

where _D_ is the training corpus, _M_ represents randomly masked positions, and _x[M]_ is the corrupted image that is masked according to _M_.
comments.explanation: The paper describes the pre-training objective as maximizing the log-likelihood of visual tokens given a corrupted image but fails to provide the corresponding mathematical expression. The prose sets up for an equation (e.g., expectation over D of sum over M of log p(z_i | x[M])) but offers none—likely due to formatting or OCR loss. Given that this objective is central to the method, and the theoretical framing depends on it, the omission prevents readers from verifying normalization (e.g., per-token vs. total) and severs the link between the algorithmic description and the loss function. While experts may infer the form, the absence is a notable flaw in a formal methods section.

comments.title: Ambiguous reuse of weight matrix symbol W_c across tasks
comments.quote: The category probabilities are computed as softmax(avg( {_**h**[L] i}[N] i=1 _**[W]**[c]))), where _**[h]**[L] i_ is the final encoding vector of the _i_-th image patch, _**W** c ∈ R^[D][×][C]_ is a parameter matrix, and _C_ is the number of labels.
comments.explanation: The symbol **W**_c is used for two different weight matrices: in pre-training (Section 2.3), **W**_c ∈ R^{|V|×D} maps embeddings to 8192 visual token logits; in fine-tuning (Section 2.6), **W**_c ∈ R^{D×C} maps pooled embeddings to C class logits. While notation reuse across distinct phases is common, the reuse of the exact symbol without clarification risks confusion, especially during quick reading. The dimensions and transposed roles differ significantly (|V| vs. C, prediction vs. classification). Using distinct symbols (e.g., **W**_tok and **W**_cls) would improve clarity. The context ultimately resolves the ambiguity, but the practice undermines precision.

comments.title: Erroneous and unclear scaling benefit claims in text vs. table
comments.quote: BEIT384-L outperforms BEIT384 by 1 . 7. In other words, the benefits of scaling up BEIT from base to large are greater than supervised pre-training with ImageNet-22K. More importantly, comparing between BEIT384 with ViT384 that conducts supervised pre-training on ImageNet-22K, the improvements of BEIT become greater along with scaling the size from base (i.e., 0 . 6) to large (i.e., 1 . 1)
comments.explanation: The empirical claim about scaling benefits is conceptually sound—BEIT gains more from scaling than supervised models do—but the specific numbers 0.6 and 1.1 cited as improvements are inconsistent with Table 1 and lack clear derivation. BEIT384-B vs. ViT384-B yields a 0.6-point gain (84.6 - 84.0), and BEIT384-L vs. ViT384-L yields a 1.1-point gain (86.3 - 85.2), which supports the directional claim. However, the phrasing 'improvements of BEIT become greater' alongside unlabelled numbers (0.6, 1.1) risks misinterpretation as absolute gains from scaling, rather than relative improvements over supervised baselines. The numbers appear to be placeholders or OCR artifacts, undermining credibility and clarity in an important argument about model scaling.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing in 'recover 100% visual tokens' ablation
comments.quote: +Recover 100%visual tokens
comments.explanation: The ablation name '+Recover 100% visual tokens' misleadingly suggests an enhancement to the BEIT framework, but it actually refers to removing masking and training the model to reconstruct all tokens—departing from standard MIM. This contradicts the core self-supervised design where only masked positions are predicted. The '+' notation implies additive improvement, but the result shows performance degradation. The correct interpretation appears to be '-Masking + Full Reconstruction', but the label obscures this. While context clarifies the intent, the naming is logically inconsistent and could mislead readers into thinking full reconstruction is a viable or beneficial extension.

comments.title: Misrendered layer-wise output rescaling factor
comments.quote: we rescale the output matrices ... by ~~_√_~~ 12 _l_.
comments.explanation: The rescaling factor '12 _l_' appears to be an OCR corruption of '1/\sqrt{l}', a common stabilization technique in deep Transformers where outputs are scaled by the inverse square root of layer depth. The stated purpose—training stabilization—aligns with attenuation via 1/\sqrt{l}, whereas scaling by 12l would amplify activations with depth and destabilize training. The literal text is therefore contradictory and misleading. Given that this detail affects implementation and training dynamics, the error could lead to misinterpretation or faulty replication. The intended meaning is recoverable from context, but the presentation is faulty.

comments.title: Inconsistent pre-training duration claim
comments.quote: The pre-training runs for about 500k steps (i.e., 800 epochs)
comments.explanation: The equivalence of 500k steps to 800 epochs on ImageNet-1K with a batch size of 2k is mathematically inconsistent. ImageNet-1K contains ~1.2M images; at batch size 2k, one epoch is ~600 steps. Thus, 500k steps equals approximately 833 epochs, not 800. While close, this 4% discrepancy may mislead readers about convergence rates or training efficiency, especially in ablation studies comparing pre-training length. The number 800 may be rounded, but stating '500k steps (i.e., 800 epochs)' presents it as exact, which is inaccurate without further clarification (e.g., dataset filtering or variable batch sizes). This undermines precision in reporting experimental setup.

comments.title: Ambiguous aspect ratio sampling in blockwise masking algorithm
comments.quote: _r ←_ Rand(0 _._ 3, 0 _._ 3[)] _▷ Aspect ratio of block
comments.explanation: The notation 'Rand(0.3, 0.3)' suggests sampling from an interval where both bounds are identical, which would produce a constant value of 0.3, not a random aspect ratio as intended. This contradicts the description of blockwise masking that aims to randomly choose an aspect ratio. In Algorithm 1, this step is meant to introduce variability in block shape, but if r is fixed at 0.3, the masking blocks lose this property. Comparing with standard practice (e.g., in Bao et al., 2020 or Raffel et al., 2020), aspect ratios are typically sampled over a range (e.g., 0.3 to 3.0 or similar). Given OCR artifacts noted in the instructions, this may be a scanning error where the upper bound was misread (e.g., perhaps it was '3.0' originally). Even assuming the typo, the absence of a valid sampling range weakens the clarity of the algorithm and leaves the masking strategy underspecified.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about recoverability of original image from ELBO derivation
comments.quote: Considering the evidence lower bound (ELBO) of the log-likelihood _p_ ( _x|x_ ˜), i.e., recovering the original image from its corrupted version:
comments.explanation: The paper claims that the ELBO is for _p(x|x̃)_ — recovering the full original image from the corrupted (masked) input. But BEIT does not recover the full image; it only predicts visual tokens for masked positions, which are a bottleneck representation, not a full pixel-level reconstruction. Moreover, the term _pψ(x|z)_ is described as 'decodes the image' — referring to the dVAE decoder — which is fixed and not learned during BEIT pre-training. Thus, BEIT does not directly optimize a lower bound on _p(x|x̃)_, but rather on a simpler token prediction objective. The variational perspective is insightful, but this framing overreaches by suggesting the model estimates a full image density. A more accurate interpretation would link MIM to a conditional token prediction under a pretrained prior, not full image recovery.

comments.title: Underspecified block size calculation in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: The OCR damage is evident here: the symbols '√' and '' appear to be corruption of mathematical operations. Context suggests these are meant to be _a ← √(s) · r_ and _b ← √(s) / r_, or perhaps _a ← sqrt(s × r)_ and _b ← sqrt(s / r)_, commonly used to compute spatial dimensions from block area. However, the current text is unreadable and unauditable. While one can infer intent from standard implementations (e.g., in MAE or BeiT codebases), the algorithm as presented is irreproducible without guessing the missing operations. Given that Algorithm 1 is central to the method's novelty (blockwise masking), this clarity issue is significant. Even assuming plausible reconstructions, the notation lacks units and fails to specify how non-integer values are handled (e.g., floor/ceil).

The rest of the section uses the correct idea: visual tokens are discrete indices. Therefore, while the core concept is sound and used correctly elsewhere, the notation here is malformed and could confuse a detail-oriented reader trying to formalize the input-output structure.

It appears the authors intended to define: (1) pψ(x|z) as the decoder (image reconstruction from tokens), (2) pθ(z|˜x) as the encoder (predicting tokens from masked image), and (3) qφ(z|x) as the image tokenizer. However, as written, the clauses are fused incorrectly: '(2) _pθ_ ( _z|x_ ˜) recovers the visual tokens based on the masked _pψ_ ( _x|z_ ) decodes the image...' suggests that pψ is part of pθ, which is nonsensical. More importantly, comparing between BEIT384 with ViT384 that conducts supervised pre-training on ImageNet-22K, the improvements of BEIT become greater along with scaling the size from base (i.e., 0 . 6) to large (i.e., 1 . 1)
comments.explanation: The statement claims that BEIT384-L outperforms BEIT384 by 1.7 points (from 84.6 to 86.3 in Table 1), and that this improvement (1.7) exceeds the gain from supervised pre-training on ImageNet-22K (which is 84.0 - 83.1 = 0.9 for ViT384-B). So far, this is consistent. However, the text then says 'the improvements of BEIT become greater along with scaling the size from base (i.e., 0.6) to large (i.e., 1.1)', but these numbers (0.6 and 1.1) do not match any apparent performance deltas in the table. The only interpretation is that 0.6 refers to BEIT384-B (84.6) minus DeiT384-B (83.1) = 1.5, or perhaps BEIT-B minus MoCo v3-B = 83.2 - 83.2 = 0, which doesn't fit. The 0.6 and 1.1 appear to be placeholders or OCR errors (maybe confused with other results). Given that BEIT-L (85.2) vs. BEIT-B (83.2) is a 2.0 jump, and BEIT384-L (86.3) vs. BEIT384-B (84.6) is 1.7, while supervised large (ViT384-L) gives only 1.2 more than base (85.2 - 84.0), the claim about relative gains from scaling is conceptually sound. But the specific numbers 0.6 and 1.1 have no clear origin and appear to be erroneous or mis-transcribed. This introduces confusion about the magnitude of gains.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'recover 100% visual tokens' ablation
comments.quote: +Recover 100%visual tokens
comments.explanation: This ablation variant suggests training the model to predict all visual tokens rather than only the masked ones. However, in standard MIM (like BERT), only masked positions are predicted, so recovering 100% would fundamentally change the task and potentially introduce information leakage during pre-training. The model is not designed to predict unmasked tokens. While Table 4 shows performance degradation (82.59 vs. 82.86 in classification), the phrasing '+Recover 100% visual tokens' implies it's an additive change to BEIT, but in context, it likely means removing masking entirely and training to reconstruct all tokens (as in Table 4's next row: '-Masking+Recover 100%visual tokens'). The notation '+Recover...' is misleading—it should be '-Masking' or similar. Since the control model uses masking, removing it is a subtraction, not an addition. This contradicts standard self-supervised practice and could mislead readers into thinking full reconstruction is a simple extension. Context resolves the general intent (it's worse), but the '+' label is logically inconsistent.

comments.title: Missing justification for blockwise masking implementation detail
comments.quote: r ← Rand(0 . 3, 0 . 3[)] _▷ Aspect ratio of block a ← ~~_[√]_~~ s · r ; b ← ~~~~ s/r
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the aspect ratio `r` is sampled from `Rand(0.3, 0.3)`, which implies a fixed value of 0.3 rather than a range. This contradicts the comment 'Randomly choose an aspect ratio' and the intended behavior described in text ('randomly choose an aspect ratio for the masking block'). The OCR may have corrupted the upper bound (e.g., perhaps it was meant to be `Rand(0.3, 3.0)` or similar). A fixed aspect ratio of 0.3 would make all blocks very tall and skinny (e.g., if s=49, then a=√49×0.3≈2.1, b≈49/(2.1)≈23.3), which is unlikely. Standard practice (e.g., in BEiT and later work) uses variable aspect ratios. While the paper elsewhere states they use blockwise masking and results are good, this algorithmic detail is underspecified and appears broken as written. No clarification is given for why aspect ratio is fixed at 0.3. This makes reproducibility difficult.

comments.title: Inconsistent weight matrix dimension in classification description
comments.quote: softmax(avg( {_**h**^[L]_i}^N_{i=1) **W**_c}) , where ... **W**_c ∈ R^{D×C}
comments.explanation: The classification formula is written as `softmax(avg({h^[L]_i}) W_c)`, with `W_c ∈ R^{D×C}`. This is correct: avg pooling yields a vector in R^D, and multiplying by W_c (D×C) gives logits in R^C, then softmax. However, earlier in the passage under '2.3 Pre-training', it states: '_**W** c ∈ R [|V|×][D]_' in the MIM objective. This is also correct — there, W_c is for predicting visual tokens over |V|=8192 classes, so it should be |V|×D. The issue is that the same symbol **W_c** is used for two different weight matrices: one in MIM (|V|×D), one in classification (D×C). While mathematically fine (different tasks), this overloading is confusing and risks misinterpretation, especially for readers skimming. A better practice would be distinct notation (e.g., **W_pred** and **W_cls**). The context resolves the correct use in each section, but the reuse of **W_c** is inconsistent and warrants clarification.

comments.title: Unclear direction of comparison in scaling benefit claim
comments.quote: comparing between BEIT384 with ViT384 that conducts supervised pre-training on ImageNet-22K, the improvements of BEIT become greater along with scaling the size from base (i.e., 0 . 6) to large (i.e., 1 . 1)
comments.explanation: The sentence attempts to argue that BEIT's relative gain over supervised pre-training increases when moving from base to large models. However, as previously noted, the numbers 0.6 and 1.1 are unexplained and don't align with the table. Moreover, BEIT384-L achieves 86.3 vs. ViT384-L's 85.2: a 1.1-point gain. But BEIT384-B (84.6) vs. ViT384-B (84.0) is only a 0.6-point gain — so the *relative improvement* does increase with scale, which supports the claim. However, without explicit numbers or a clear reference, a reader could easily misread this as claiming BEIT gains 1.1 absolute points more at large scale, which isn't accurately reflected. The intent is valid — BEIT benefits more from scaling than supervised methods — but the ambiguous phrasing and unclear numbers introduce risk of overstatement.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Layer-wise Output Rescaling Formula
comments.quote: we rescale the output matrices (i.e., the last linear projection within each sub-layer) of the self-attention module and the feed-forward network by ~~_√_~~ 12 _l_.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the expression '12 _l_' in the rescaling factor. Given the surrounding context and standard practices in Transformer initialization, this appears to be an OCR error where '1/√l' was incorrectly rendered. The passage says the rescaling is applied to the l-th Transformer layer, and the goal is stabilization during pre-training — this aligns with known techniques such as those in deep Transformers where scaling by 1/√l helps attenuate signal growth in deeper layers. The full paper context confirms that layer-wise output rescaling involves scaling by 1/√l. However, the text as written (with '12 l') would imply a growing scale with depth, which would destabilize training — contradicting the stated purpose. While the intended meaning is recoverable from context (especially the PAPER SUMMARY), the literal text is misleading and could cause misimplementation. The use of '12' instead of '1/' may stem from poor OCR of a slashed '1' or fraction bar.

comments.title: Inconsistent Definition of Softmax Classifier Input Dimension
comments.quote: p_MIM(z′ |x[M]) = softmax_{z′}(**W**_c **h**^[L]_i + **b**_c), where **W**_c ∈ R^{|V|×D}
comments.explanation: At first glance, this equation appears inconsistent: **W**_c is defined as R^{|V|×D}, and **h**^[L]_i ∈ R^D, so the matrix-vector product **W**_c **h**^[L]_i yields a vector in R^{|V|}, which is correct for token prediction over vocabulary size |V|. However, in Section 2.6, for the downstream classification task, **W**_c is redefined as ∈ R^{D×C}, where C is the number of classes — a transpose in both shape and purpose. This is not flagged as an error because: (1) it's common practice to reuse **W**_c notation for task-specific classifiers; (2) the downstream classification section specifies it's a different matrix; (3) the full paper context distinguishes these roles. Still, using the same symbol (**W**_c) for two differently shaped and used matrices — one |V|×D in pre-training, one D×C in fine-tuning — risks confusion, especially for readers skimming. The distinction is clarified contextually (different tasks), but naming them **W**_tok and **W**_cls would improve clarity.

comments.title: Missing Justification for One-Point Approximation in ELBO Derivation
comments.quote: We simplify qφ(**z**|xi) to a one-point distribution qφ(**z**|xi) ≈ δ(**z** − argmax qφ(**z**|xi)).
comments.explanation: The derivation in Section 2.4 presents the BEIT pre-training objective as arising from the ELBO of a VAE, where the posterior qφ(**z|x**) is approximated by a delta function at the argmax. While the paper states this is an approximation, it provides no discussion of when or why this is valid. In variational inference, replacing a full posterior with a MAP estimate can lead to underestimating uncertainty and may break the variational bound unless carefully justified. The assumption is also listed in the PAPER SUMMARY, but no empirical or theoretical argument is given for why this one-point approximation suffices — particularly in contrast to the Gumbel-softmax used during dVAE training. Given that this forms the theoretical grounding for the method, skipping justification for such a strong approximation weakens the argument. More discussion or citation (e.g., to deterministic autoencoders or straight-through estimators) would strengthen the claim.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Blockwise Masking Algorithm Aspect Ratio
comments.quote: _r ←_ Rand(0.3, 0.3[)] _▷ Aspect ratio of block
comments.explanation: This line in Algorithm 1 is clearly corrupted by OCR: 'Rand(0.3, 0.3[)]' suggests a uniform draw from an interval of zero width, which would always return 0.3. However, the comment says 'Aspect ratio of block', implying variation. From context — and standard implementations of blockwise masking like in BERT or SimMIM — the aspect ratio is typically sampled from a range (e.g., [0.3, 3.3] or [1/3, 3]). The full paper context elsewhere mentions blockwise masking improves segmentation via longer-range dependencies, which requires variable block shapes. Therefore, a fixed aspect ratio of 0.3 would undermine this motivation. While the most plausible fix is that the upper bound was meant to be 3.3 or 1/0.3, the OCR error leaves the actual sampling distribution underspecified. This could prevent reproducibility. The comment helps, but the parameters must be correctly rendered.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Pre-training Duration Claim
comments.quote: The pre-training runs for about 500k steps (i.e., 800 epochs)
comments.explanation: The claim that 500k steps equals 800 epochs on ImageNet-1K with a batch size of 2k appears inconsistent. ImageNet-1K has ~1.2M images; with batch size 2k, one epoch is ~600 steps (1.2M / 2k = 600). So 500k steps would be ~833 epochs (500k / 600), not 800. While close, it's off by ~4%. This may stem from using a slightly different dataset size (e.g., after filtering) or batch size (e.g., effective batch size), but the discrepancy is notable. The PAPER SUMMARY later states: 'Pre-training longer (800 epochs) improves downstream performance over 300 epochs' — suggesting 800 is a rounded number. Still, equating 500k steps to exactly 800 epochs implies ~625 images per step on average, which would require a batch size of ~1.5k. Given that both numbers are presented as precise, the mismatch could mislead readers about training efficiency or convergence rates, especially in ablation studies comparing pre-training length.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a timely and well-motivated contribution to the self-supervised learning literature for vision Transformers. The proposal of a BERT-style masked image modeling (MIM) task is a conceptually elegant bridge between successful NLP paradigms and computer vision, and the introduction is commendably clear in articulating the central challenge—the lack of a natural image vocabulary—and convincingly arguing for the proposed solution of using discrete 'visual tokens' over naive pixel regression. However, the most significant issue that emerges from this initial section is the treatment of the discrete VAE tokenizer as a given prerequisite. The paper's core contribution is somewhat dependent on this separate, non-trivial module, and its overall impact would be significantly strengthened by a more thorough analysis of the tokenizer's role, including its sensitivity to architectural choices, training data, and computational cost. Without this, the effectiveness of the MIM framework itself risks being conflated with the quality of this external component, a point that requires careful unpacking in the full paper.

comments.title: Ambiguous masking notation in Section 2.3
comments.quote: x[M] = {x_p_i : i ∉ M} ∪ {e_M : i ∈ M}
comments.explanation: The notation for constructing the masked image has two issues: (1) The first set {x_p_i : i ∉ M} doesn't specify the domain of i, which should be i ∈ {1,...,N} \ M. (2) The second set {e_M : i ∈ M} would create multiple copies of the same mask embedding e_M for each masked position, when it should be placing a single e_M at each masked position. The authors likely meant to replace each masked patch with e_M, but the set notation is misleading. This should be written more clearly as defining a sequence where position i contains x_p_i if i ∉ M and e_M if i ∈ M.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimension notation for visual tokens in Section 2.1.2
comments.quote: we tokenize the image x ∈ R^{H×W×C} into z = [z₁, ..., z_N] ∈ V^{h×w}
comments.explanation: The visual tokens z are described both as a sequence [z₁, ..., z_N] and as belonging to V^{h×w}. While mathematically equivalent since h×w = N (as mentioned later), this dual notation is confusing. A sequence [z₁, ..., z_N] naturally belongs to V^N, not V^{h×w}. The authors should consistently use either sequence notation or grid notation, not mix them in the same definition. This could confuse readers about the structure of the token representation.

comments.title: Ambiguous formula notation for classification probabilities
comments.quote: The category probabilities are computed as softmax(avg( { h^L_i }_{i=1}^N W_c ))
comments.explanation: The formula shows W_c (the weight matrix) inside the avg() function, which is mathematically incorrect. The avg() operates on the hidden vectors h^L_i (each in R^D), producing a D-dimensional vector. The weight matrix W_c ∈ R^{D×C} should multiply this averaged representation to produce a C-dimensional output for the softmax. The correct formula should be softmax(W_c * avg({h^L_i}_{i=1}^N)) or softmax(avg({h^L_i}_{i=1}^N) * W_c). While the intended meaning is clear from context, the notation as written is technically incorrect and could confuse readers.

comments.title: OCR artifacts in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: The mathematical operations for calculating block dimensions a and b contain OCR artifacts (~~_[√]_~~ and ~~~~). Based on the context of calculating rectangular block dimensions from size s and aspect ratio r, these should likely be square root operations: a ← √(s·r) and b ← √(s/r). These artifacts make the algorithm unclear and could prevent reproduction.

comments.title: Potential aspect ratio range error in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: _r ←_ Rand(0 _. 3, 0 _. 3[)] _· Aspect ratio of block
comments.explanation: The algorithm specifies r ← Rand(0.3, 0.3), which would always return 0.3, making the aspect ratio fixed rather than variable. This contradicts the stated goal of randomly choosing an aspect ratio. Given the context and standard practices in masking algorithms, this is likely an OCR error where the intended range was (0.3, 3.33) or similar to allow for both landscape and portrait orientations. The authors clearly intend variable aspect ratios based on the comment and the surrounding logic, but as written, the algorithm would not achieve this.

comments.title: Undefined notation in objective function
comments.quote: where _D_ is the training corpus, _M_ represents randomly masked positions, and _x[M]_ is the corrupted image that is masked according to _M_ .
comments.explanation: The objective function uses x[M] to denote the corrupted image, but this notation was not explicitly defined in the preceding text. While x[M] was used earlier in the section to describe the corrupted patches, the objective function appears to reference this without clear definition. A careful reader can infer the meaning from context, but formal mathematical writing would typically define all terms before using them in equations. This is a minor clarity issue that doesn't affect understanding.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in masking ratio description
comments.quote: We randomly mask at most 75 patches (i.e., roughly 40% of total image patches).
comments.explanation: With a 224×224 image and 16×16 patches, there are 14×14 = 196 total patches. 75 out of 196 patches is 38.3%, not 'roughly 40%'. While this is a small discrepancy (1.7% difference), stating 'roughly 40%' when the actual maximum is 38.3% is slightly misleading. The authors could either adjust the patch count to 78 (which would be 39.8%, closer to 40%) or adjust the percentage to 'roughly 38%'.

comments.title: Minor numerical discrepancy in epochs calculation
comments.quote: The pre-training runs for about 500k steps (i.e., 800 epochs) with 2k batch size.
comments.explanation: With 1.2M images and a batch size of 2k, each epoch consists of 1.2M/2k = 600 steps. Therefore, 500k steps corresponds to 500k/600 ≈ 833 epochs, not 800 epochs. While the 'about' qualifier provides some flexibility, the 4% difference (833 vs 800) is notable. The authors could either adjust the step count to 480k (which would be 800 epochs) or adjust the epoch count to approximately 833.

comments.title: Missing word in classification description
comments.quote: Specifically, we use average pooling to aggregate the representations, and feed the global to a softmax classifier.
comments.explanation: The sentence is incomplete - it should read 'feed the global representation' or 'feed the global features' to a softmax classifier. This is a minor grammatical issue that doesn't affect understanding, but it's worth noting for clarity.

comments.title: Ambiguous terminology for ViT prediction target
comments.quote: ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) conducts a preliminary exploration with the masked patch prediction task, which predicts the 3-bit mean color of the masked patches.
comments.explanation: The text describes iGPT as using a '9-bit color palette' and ViT as predicting '3-bit mean color'. In the original ViT paper (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020), the masked patch prediction task quantizes the average color to '3 bits per channel' (effectively 9-bit color). Using '3-bit' here is ambiguous and potentially misleading, as it suggests a much lower resolution (8 colors) compared to iGPT's 512 colors, whereas both methods likely use a similar or identical color resolution (3 bits per channel). This inconsistency could lead readers to incorrectly believe ViT's prediction target is significantly coarser than iGPT's.

comments.title: Imprecise terminology for attention visualization
comments.quote: The visualizations are produced by attention scores computed via query-key product in the last layer.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'attention scores' (typically the unnormalized logits from the query-key product) for visualization. However, visualizations of self-attention mechanisms almost exclusively use 'attention weights'—the normalized probabilities resulting from the softmax operation. Visualizing raw scores is non-standard and would not effectively represent 'which patch it attends to' in a probabilistic sense. This is likely a terminology slip where 'weights' was intended.

comments.title: Vague quantitative claim regarding efficiency
comments.quote: In comparison, BEIT can achieve several times of improvement in terms of pre-training throughput (Appendix E), and memory consumption.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'several times' improvement in throughput and memory over contrastive learning methods is strong and somewhat vague without immediate quantitative support in the text (it references an Appendix). While plausible given architectural differences (single view vs. two views), 'several' usually implies a factor greater than 2. Without the data from Appendix E immediately visible to support this specific magnitude, the claim appears potentially exaggerated or imprecise for a rigorous comparison.
