progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper presents a conceptually elegant and well-motivated adaptation of BERT-style masked language modeling to the vision domain, addressing the inherent challenges of pixel-based auto-encoding by introducing discrete visual tokens. The writing is clear, logical, and follows a standard, effective structure for high-impact computer vision research, moving seamlessly from the limitations of previous "data-hungry" vision Transformers to a robust, proposed solution. The most significant strength is the clarity of the core innovation—the pivot from predicting raw pixels (which often leads to a focus on high-frequency noise) to predicting latent visual tokens, which effectively forces the model to capture semantic structure. If there is a potential pitfall in the study, it lies in the complexity of the "two-stage" pipeline; the reliance on an external, pre-learned dVAE tokenizer introduces specific hyper-parameters and architectural dependencies that require thorough ablation to ensure the performance gains are derived from the MIM task itself rather than the quality of the discrete visual codebook. Overall, the work is highly professional, addresses a critical bottleneck in self-supervised learning, and provides a compelling framework for future representation learning in vision.

comments.title: Inconsistent mathematical definition of Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)
comments.quote: where (1)original image given input visual tokens; (3) qφ ( z|x ) denotes the image tokenizer that obtains visual tokens; (2) pθ ( z|x˜ ) recovers the visual tokens based on the masked pψ ( x|z ) decodes the image, which is our MIM pre-training task.
comments.explanation: The text in section 2.4 attempts to define the components of the ELBO, but the labels (1), (2), and (3) are mangled and nested within the prose confusingly. Specifically, the text states 'pψ ( x|z ) decodes the image, which is our MIM pre-training task.' This is mathematically incorrect according to the logic established earlier; the MIM pre-training task is to predict the visual tokens (z) from the masked image (x˜), which corresponds to the term pθ(z|x˜), not the decoder pψ(x|z). The decoder is the component of the dVAE, whereas the MIM objective is the learned prior over tokens.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for indexing and sets in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the indices (i, j) are used to define the masked block coordinates, but it is not explicitly stated whether these refer to the grid indices (e.g., in the 14x14 grid) or pixel coordinates. Given the input is 'N (= h x w) image patches', the indexing loop should strictly operate in grid-coordinate space. While context suggests this, the notation 'i ∈ [t, t+a)' where a is a derived block size strongly implies discrete grid indices, but the lack of formal index ranges (e.g., 0 ≤ i < h) makes the algorithm slightly underspecified for a reader attempting a clean reproduction.

comments.title: Potential conflict between text and equation for input embeddings
comments.quote: The input vectors H 0 = [ e [S] , Ex [p] i [ , . . . ,] Ex [p] N [ +] E [pos] [ is fed] into Transformer.
comments.explanation: The notation 'Ex [p] i [ , . . . ,] Ex [p] N [ +] E [pos]' is confusing due to the OCR artifact/formatting. Standard Transformer notation usually implies the position embedding is added to the patch embeddings prior to concatenation with the special token [S]. The current expression makes it appear as if E_pos is only added to the last patch or the sequence as a whole in an ill-defined way. While the conceptual intent (ViT standard) is clear, the formal expression is syntactically ambiguous resulting from the concatenation of indices and operators.

comments.explanation: The text block describing the VAE formulation contains severe OCR-related corruption. While the intent is to explain the transition from the general ELBO to the specific BEIT objective (approximating the posterior $q_\phi(z|x)$ as a one-point distribution), the sentence structure has collapsed, creating nonsense phrases such as 'learn the priorwith', followed by a broken mathematical assignment involving $\hat{q_\phi}$ and stray symbols. This obscures the derivation of the objective function provided in Section 2.4.

comments.title: Inconsistent Initialization Scaling Factor
comments.quote: rescale the output matrices ... by $\sqrt{1 / 2l}$.
comments.explanation: The text contains a LaTeX artifact '$\sqrt{12l}$' which appears to be an OCR misread of a standard initialization heuristic. In Transformer literature (e.g., GPT-2 or specific Vision Transformer initializations), layer-wise scaling is typically $\sqrt{1 / (2L)}$ or similar based on the layer index $l$. Given the context of stabilizing deep training, the notation '$\sqrt{12l}$' (square root of 12 times l) is almost certainly a misinterpretation of '$\sqrt{1/(2l)}$'. While common in OCR errors, it is problematic as a 'method' description because a reader attempting to reproduce the initialization would apply a factor that grows with the layer index rather than shrinks, which is detrimental to training stability.

comments.title: Inconsistent Scaling Factor in Initialization
comments.quote: rescale the output matrices (i.e., the last linear projection within each sub-layer) of the self-attention module and the feed-forward network by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2l}}$.
comments.explanation: In Section 2.5, the authors specify a layer-wise rescaling factor of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2l}}$, where $l$ is the layer index ($l=1, \dots, L$). In deep learning initialization papers (like the original Xavier/He or more recent Transformer stability papers), the standard practice for depth-scaling typically involves $\frac{1}{\sqrt{L}}$ or $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2L}}$ (constant per layer) or $\frac{1}{\sqrt{l}}$ (progressive). Using $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2l}}$ means that as $l$ increases, the magnitude of the weights decreases significantly. In a 12-layer model ($L=12$), the factor for the final layer would be $\frac{1}{\sqrt{24}} \approx 0.2$. This is an aggressive attenuation. While this might be the authors' empirical finding, the notation $1/\sqrt{2l}$ implies a dependency on $l$ that is non-standard. If the authors intended $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2L}}$ (constant scale), the notation is misleading. If they truly meant $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2l}}$, the rapid drop-off in scaling by the final layers is mathematically unusual for typical Transformer stability and warrants clarification whether this was a typo for $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2L}}$.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Masking Ratio in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: $s \leftarrow \text{Rand}(16, 0.4N - |M|)$
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the variable $s$ represents the block size, chosen randomly. The upper bound of the range is given as $0.4N - |M|$. While this logic is intended to ensure we approach the 40% threshold, it is technically problematic if $|M|$ is already close to $0.4N$, as the upper bound could become smaller than the lower bound (16), potentially causing errors in the `Rand` function implementation. Additionally, the algorithm uses $0.4N$ as a total target, but the text states: 'randomly mask approximately 40% image patches'. It is unclear if the algorithm should terminate strictly at $0.4N$ or if it allows slightly more patches. The logic `until |M| > 0.4N` suggests it will always exceed 40%, which contradicts 'approximately 40%'.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in mask indexing
comments.quote: where the masked positions are denoted as $M \in\{ 1 , \dots , N \}[0] .[4] [N] .$
comments.explanation: The notation $M \in \{1, \dots, N\}^{0.4N}$ is mathematically unusual and potentially confusing. Since $M$ is defined as a subset of patch positions, it should denote a subset $M \subset \{1, \dots, N\}$ such that $|M| \approx 0.4N$. The current notation looks like a typo or an OCR misread of an exponent or a set-builder notation. Given the algorithm defined later in section 2.3 accurately describes the process, this is likely a notation error that could mislead a reader about the structure of $M$.

comments.title: Potential sign error in ELBO equation
comments.quote: $\\log p(x | x^{(M)}) \\ge \\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z|x)} [\\log p_\psi(x|z)] + \\sum_{i \in M} \\log p_\theta(z_i | x^{(M)})$
comments.explanation: The ELBO reformulation presented in the summary claims $\log p(x|x^{(M)}) \ge \mathbb{E}[\dots] + \sum \log p(\dots)$. However, in standard variational inference theory, the goal is often to maximize the sum of terms (the evidence lower bound). The provided equation appears to be a heuristic simplification rather than a standard mathematical derivation. While the authors define the objective in Section 2.4 more clearly using the reconstruction loss, the equation displayed in the summary section (as derived from the paper's intent to reformulate log-likelihood) is mathematically ambiguous and likely missing the negative sign typical of loss-based ELBO formulations or proper probability distribution notations.

comments.title: Ambiguity in layer rescaling factor
comments.quote: the $l$-th Transformer layer, output matrices in self-attention/feed-forward modules are scaled by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2l}}$.
comments.explanation: The text states the scaling factor as $1 / \sqrt{2l}$. In standard deep Transformer implementations (like those for stability in large-scale training), one often sees scaling by $1/\sqrt{l}$ or other constant factors per layer to maintain variance. Testing for $l=1$: the factor is $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$. The inclusion of the '2' in the denominator is non-standard. While the authors may have empirically found this specific constant effective for stability, the notation is slightly ambiguous as to whether it is $1/\sqrt{2l}$ or $(1/\sqrt{2}) * l$ or similar. Given the paper's emphasis on stability, this is a significant implementation detail that should be explicit.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript introduces BEIT, a self-supervised pre-training framework for vision Transformers that successfully adapts BERT-style masked language modeling to computer vision via a masked image modeling (MIM) task. The core idea—predicting discrete visual tokens (extracted via a discrete VAE) for randomly masked image patches—is well-motivated, clearly articulated, and addresses a legitimate gap in the literature regarding denoising auto-encoding for vision architectures. The writing is generally accessible, and the introduction effectively contextualizes the work within contrastive and self-distillation paradigms while highlighting the novelty of bidirectional, token-level recovery. However, the prose requires careful polishing to resolve minor grammatical inconsistencies, redundant phrasing between the abstract and introduction, and imprecise terminology (e.g., “pre-exist vocabulary,” slightly awkward syntactic constructions). More critically, the manuscript would benefit from a more rigorous upfront discussion of the discrete tokenizer’s integration into the end-to-end objective, including how it mitigates potential distributional shifts or computational overhead, as well as a substantive elaboration of the claimed variational autoencoder connection, which is currently only listed as a contribution. Overall, the work presents a timely and conceptually sound direction, but sharpening the methodological exposition, refining the prose, and deepening

comments.title: Missing ELBO Equation and Jumbled Theoretical References
comments.quote:
Considering the evidence lower bound (ELBO) of the log-likelihood _p_ ( _x|x_ ˜), i.e., recovering the original image from its corrupted version:

where (1)original image given input visual tokens; (3) _qφ_ ( _z|x_ ) denotes the image tokenizer that obtains visual tokens; (2) _pθ_ ( _z|x_ ˜) recovers the visual tokens based on the masked _pψ_ ( _x|z_ ) decodes the image, which is our MIM pre-training task.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce the ELBO decomposition to theoretically justify BEIT's pre-training as a variational autoencoder task (matching the claim in the Contributions). However, the actual ELBO equation is completely missing, likely dropped during PDF column extraction. The subsequent text attempts to label components (1), (2), and (3), but the references are jumbled and grammatically broken. Without the equation, it is impossible to verify how the MIM loss maps to the reconstruction term of the ELBO or how the KL divergence term is handled. This is critical to the paper's theoretical contribution.

comments.title: Missing Pre-Training Objective Formula
comments.quote:
The pre-training objective is to maximize the log-likelihood of the correct visual tokens _zi_ given the corrupted image:

where _D_ is the training corpus, _M_ represents randomly masked positions, and _x[M]_ is the corrupted image that is masked according to _M_ .
comments.explanation: Following the statement about maximizing log-likelihood, the explicit mathematical formulation of the cross-entropy loss is absent. The text immediately jumps to defining the dataset and masked positions. While the paragraph describes using a softmax classifier with weights _W_c_ and bias _b_c_, the objective function itself (likely a sum of cross-entropy losses over masked positions) is not rendered. This gap prevents a clear understanding of the exact optimization target and how batch/corpus normalization is applied.

comments.quote: In the second stage, we learn the priorwith the most likely visual tokens _pθ_ while keeping _z_ ˆ _qi φ_ = arg max and _pψ_ fixed. _z qφ_ We simplify( _z|xi_ ). Then Equation (2) can be rewritten as: _qφ_ ( **z** _|xi_ ) to a one-point distribution
comments.explanation: The paragraph describing the transition from a probabilistic VAE to a deterministic discrete tokenizer is severely garbled. The intended logic appears to be collapsing the posterior _q_φ(z|x) into a one-point distribution (a Dirac delta at the mode _z_ = argmax _q_φ(z|x)) to simplify the ELBO and remove the KL divergence term, which aligns with standard discrete VAE practices (e.g., VQ-VAE). However, the broken syntax obscures how _p_θ is learned, what happens to _z_ ˆ, and why the prior becomes trivial. The mathematical steps necessary to show this simplification are lost, leaving the method underspecified.

comments.title: Degenerate aspect ratio range in masking algorithm
comments.quote: _r ←_ Rand(0 _._ 3, 0 _._ 3[)] _▷ Aspect ratio of block
comments.explanation: Sampling an aspect ratio from a uniform distribution `Rand(0.3, 0.3)` yields a constant value of 0.3, which contradicts the stated goal of 'randomly choosing an aspect ratio' and defeats the purpose of blockwise masking (which typically aims for varied rectangular shapes to capture spatial context). This is likely an OCR error or a typo for a wider range (e.g., `[0.3, 0.3]` might have been `[0.3, 0.8]` or `[0.3, 3.0]` in the source). Regardless, a degenerate range is underspecified and likely incorrect. Readers attempting to reproduce the masking strategy would fail to generate the intended varied block shapes.

comments.title: Non-standard/ambiguous notation for masked positions
comments.quote: where the masked positions are denoted as _M ∈{_ 1 _, . . . , N }_[0] _[.]_[4] _[N]_ .
comments.explanation: The notation $M \in \{1, \dots, N\}^{0.4N}$ is non-standard and ambiguous. Typically, a subset of a specific size is denoted as $M \subset \{1, \dots, N\}$ with $|M| = 0.4N$, or using combinatorial notation. The exponent format here could be misinterpreted as a sequence of length $0.4N$ or a power set operation. While the intent (masking ~40% of patches) is clear from the surrounding text and Algorithm 1, the mathematical notation is imprecise and could confuse a careful reader parsing the formalism.

comments.title: Missing index in softmax classifier probability definition
comments.quote: predict the corresponding visual tokens _p_ MIM( _z[′] |x[M]_ ) = softmax _z′_ ( _**W** c_ _**h**[L] i_[+] _**[b]**[c]_[)][,
comments.explanation: The probability expression on the left-hand side, $p_{\text{MIM}}(z'|\mathbf{x}_M)$, does not carry the index $i$ corresponding to the specific masked patch whose representation $\mathbf{h}_i^L$ is used on the right-hand side. As written, it implies a single probability distribution over the entire corrupted image, whereas the model actually predicts a token for each masked location independently. Adding the subscript $i$ (e.g., $p_{\text{MIM}}(z'_i|\mathbf{x}_M)$) would clarify that the classifier operates per-patch. This is a minor formal oversight but could mislead readers trying to match the input/output dimensions of the prediction head.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in position embeddings
comments.quote: The input vectors _**H**_ 0 = [ _**e**_ [S] _,_ _**Ex**[p] i[, . . . ,]_ _**[ Ex]**[p] N_[] +] _**[ E]**[pos]_[ is fed] into Transformer.
comments.explanation: The text defines the position embeddings as $\mathbf{E}^{pos} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times D}$, but the input sequence prepends a special token $[S]$, making the actual sequence length $N+1$. Adding an $N \times D$ matrix to an $(N+1) \times D$ tensor is mathematically invalid. Standard Transformer implementations either use $N+1$ learnable position embeddings or append a separate learnable token for $[S]$'s position. The authors likely simplified the notation for brevity, but as stated, it contains a dimension mismatch that would fail in implementation.

comments.explanation: The notation used to define the corrupted sequence $\mathbf{x}_M$ employs a set union symbol (or similar, rendered as `` due to OCR) between two indexed families. A sequence is an ordered tuple, not a set, so set operations do not correctly describe the replacement of specific indices in a sequence. Furthermore, the notation mixes set-builder notation with sequence indexing in a way that is mathematically imprecise. While the intent (replacing patches at indices in $M$ with $\mathbf{e}_M$) is clear from context, the formal definition should use element-wise specification or sequence masking notation to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Inconsistent comparison in ablation study vs. Table 1 data
comments.quote: Compared with the results in Table 1, the ablation result is worse than training vision Transformer from scratch on two tasks.
comments.explanation: I initially found this statement confusing because it explicitly references Table 1 while making a claim that appears to contradict it. Upon checking the tables, Table 4 lists the pixel-level autoencoding ablation ('−Visual tokens') at 81.04% (ImageNet) and 41.38% (ADE20K). Table 1, however, explicitly reports the 'ViT384-B (training from scratch)' baseline at 77.9% on ImageNet. The ablation result is actually ~3.1 points higher than the stated scratch baseline in Table 1, not worse. The authors may have intended to compare against the DeiT-B scratch baseline (81.8%) or a different random-init setting, but the direct reference to Table 1 makes the prose factually inconsistent with the provided data. This needs clarification to avoid misleading reproducibility efforts.

comments.title: Ambiguous model shorthand in scaling analysis
comments.quote: As shown in Table 1, ViT384-L is worse than ViT384 on ImageNet, when training from scratch.
comments.explanation: The text compares 'ViT384-L' to 'ViT384' but omits the '-B' suffix for the base model. In Table 1, the scratch baseline is clearly labeled 'ViT384-B' (77.9%). While the intended comparison is obvious from context, the missing suffix creates a notation inconsistency. Subsequent sentences in the paragraph continue to use 'ViT384' and 'ViT384-L' without the base/large distinction, which could cause a careful reader to momentarily question whether they are comparing the correct model sizes or referring to an undefined architecture. Adding the '-B' suffix would align the prose exactly with Table 1's labeling convention.

comments.title: Non-standard/ambiguous notation for masked positions

comments.explanation: The notation $M \in \{1, \dots, N\}^{0.4N}$ is non-standard and ambiguous. While the intent (masking ~40% of patches) is clear from the surrounding text and Algorithm 1, the mathematical notation is imprecise and could confuse a careful reader parsing the formalism.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in position embeddings
comments.quote: The input vectors _**H**_ 0 = [ _**e**_ [S] _,_ _**Ex**[p] i[, . . . ,]_ _**[ Ex]**[p] N_[] +] _**[ E]**[pos]_[ is fed] into Transformer.

comments.explanation: Sampling an aspect ratio from a uniform distribution `Rand(0.3, 0.3)` yields a constant value of 0.3, which contradicts the stated goal of 'randomly choosing an aspect ratio' and defeats the purpose of blockwise masking (which typically aims for varied rectangular shapes to capture spatial context). comments.title: Inconsistent comparison in ablation study vs. Table 1 data
comments.quote: Compared with the results in Table 1, the ablation result is worse than training vision Transformer from scratch on two tasks.
comments.explanation: I initially found this statement confusing because it explicitly references Table 1 while making a claim that appears to contradict it. Upon checking the tables, Table 4 lists the pixel-level autoencoding ablation ('−Visual tokens') at 81.04% (ImageNet) and 41.38% (ADE20K). Table 1, however, explicitly reports the 'ViT384-B (training from scratch)' baseline at 77.9% on ImageNet. The ablation result is actually ~3.1 points higher than the stated scratch baseline in Table 1, not worse. The authors may have intended to compare against the DeiT-B scratch baseline (81.8%) or a different random-init setting, but the direct reference to Table 1 makes the prose factually inconsistent with the provided data. This needs clarification to avoid misleading reproducibility efforts.

comments.title: Ambiguous model shorthand in scaling analysis
comments.quote: As shown in Table 1, ViT384-L is worse than ViT384 on ImageNet, when training from scratch.
comments.explanation: The text compares 'ViT384-L' to 'ViT384' but omits the '-B' suffix for the base model. In Table 1, the scratch baseline is clearly labeled 'ViT384-B' (77.9%). While the intended comparison is obvious from context, the missing suffix creates a notation inconsistency. Subsequent sentences in the paragraph continue to use 'ViT384' and 'ViT384-L' without the base/large distinction, which could cause a careful reader to momentarily question whether they are comparing the correct model sizes or referring to an undefined architecture. Adding the '-B' suffix would align the prose exactly with Table 1's labeling convention.

comments.title: Imprecise description of attention map computation
comments.quote: The visualizations are produced by attention scores computed via query-key product in the last layer.
comments.explanation: The authors state that attention maps are computed via 'query-key product'. In standard self-attention mechanisms, attention scores are typically computed as the softmax-normalized dot product of queries and keys (often with a scaling factor, e.g., $QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k}$). Describing the computation merely as a 'query-key product' omits the softmax normalization (and scaling), meaning the resulting values are attention logits rather than proper probability distributions. While this is a common shorthand in analysis sections, a careful reader might assume the visualizations show normalized attention weights. If the authors plotted raw logits without normalization, the relative intensities in the maps could be misleading or would require an additional normalization step not mentioned here.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling methodological contribution to self-supervised learning for Vision Transformers by adapting the Masked Image Modeling (MIM) framework from NLP to the visual domain. The authors articulate a clear motivation, contrasting the limitations of naive pixel-level regression with the proposed solution of recovering discrete visual tokens, which allows the model to focus on high-level semantic structure rather than low-frequency artifacts. The technical overview and contribution summary are highly clear and well-structured, establishing a strong rationale for the complexity introduced by the separate "image tokenizer" module. The primary critique from this segment, however, is the reliance on a two-stage training process; reviewers must evaluate whether the introduced complexity and potential training instability of the tokenizer learning phase are justified by the final performance gains over simpler self-supervised baselines.

comments.title: Grammatical Error in Abstract
comments.quote: Then we randomly mask some image patches and **fed** them into the backbone Transformer.
comments.explanation: In the abstract, the authors write 'fed' instead of the correct verb form 'feed'. This is a grammatical/typographical error that interrupts the flow of the sentence describing the pre-training procedure.

comments.title: Ambiguous Figure Text
comments.quote: Visual Tokens Unused During Reconstructed Pre-Training Image
comments.explanation: The OCR-extracted text labels a block of the figure as 'Visual Tokens Unused During Reconstructed Pre-Training Image'. This appears to be an error or a garbled line. In the context of Masked Image Modeling (MIM), visual tokens are explicitly used as targets to be predicted (recovered), contradicting the word 'Unused'. In the context of the image tokenizer (reconstruction stage), they are also used (as latent variables). This ambiguity could confuse a reader trying to parse the diagram regarding the role of visual tokens in the pipeline.

comments.title: Invalid Classification Formula in MIM
comments.quote: For each masked position ... we use a softmax classifier to predict the corresponding visual tokens $p_{\text{MIM}}(z' | x[M]) = \text{softmax} _z' ( \mathbf{W}^c \mathbf{h}_{L,i} + \mathbf{b}^c )$
comments.explanation: The formula presented is mathematically problematic. The variable $z'$ represents a discrete token index (a scalar, e.g., 45). Passing a scalar index directly into a softmax function ($\text{softmax}(z')$) is not valid, as softmax requires a vector of real-valued logits as input. The standard formulation for predicting a discrete token from features $\mathbf{h}_{L,i}$ is $\text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}^c \mathbf{h}_{L,i} + \mathbf{b}^c)$, which outputs a probability distribution over the vocabulary. The intended symbol is likely the standard classifier formulation, but as written, the formula implies a nonsensical operation. This is not resolved by context, as the description explicitly mentions using a 'softmax classifier' which requires the features, not the target index, as input.

comments.title: Masking Ratio Numerical Inconsistency
comments.quote: We randomly mask at most 75 patches (i.e., roughly 40% of total image patches).
comments.explanation: For a 224x224 image split into 16x16 patches, there are $14 	imes 14 = 196$ patches. Forty percent of 196 is 78.4. Masking 75 patches corresponds to approximately 38.3%. While 'roughly 40%' might be accepted as an informal estimate, the parenthetical remark '(i.e., roughly 40%)' suggests a connection between the fixed number 75 and the ratio 40%. The inconsistency (75 vs 78.4) creates a minor ambiguity regarding the actual proportion of the image masked during training, even if the description 'randomly mask at most 75' sets a clear upper bound.

comments.title: Disjointed List of Equation Components
comments.quote: where (1)original image given input visual tokens; (3) $q_\phi (z|x)$ denotes the image tokenizer that obtains visual tokens; (2) $p_\theta (z|x_{\sim})$ recovers the visual tokens based on the masked $p_\psi (x|z)$ decodes the image, which is our MIM pre-training task.
comments.explanation: This section attempts to describe the factors or terms of an Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) equation by referencing indices (1), (3), and (2) in a non-sequential and disjointed manner. The text concatenates definitions without clear transitions (e.g., 'masked $p_\psi (x|z)$'). While the definitions of the symbols ($q_\phi$, $p_\theta$, $p_\psi$) are present and logically consistent with the theory, the presentation is confusing. The jumbled numbering and sentence structure (moving from (1) to (3) then (2)) do not help the reader identify which term corresponds to which part of the mathematical decomposition, even though the underlying definitions are understandable.

comments.title: Ambiguity in attention visualization aggregation
comments.quote: The visualizations are produced by attention scores computed via query-key product in the last layer. For each reference point, we use the corresponding patch as query, and show which patch it attends to.
comments.explanation: The text describes the extraction of attention scores (specifically the query-key product) and their plotting. However, it does not specify how these scores are aggregated across the multiple attention heads present in the Transformer (e.g., averaging, selecting the most confident head, or summing). In Vision Transformers, individual attention heads often exhibit noisy or varying patterns that do not clearly separate objects; semantic separation is typically observed in the aggregated attention matrix. Without specifying the aggregation method, the reader cannot determine exactly which heatmap is being presented or how the 'object separation' property is formally verified.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: As an academic reviewer, I would assess this paper as a high-quality and well-structured contribution that addresses a timely and important problem in self-supervised vision learning. The clarity is strong: the motivation for adapting BERT-style pre-training to vision is clearly articulated, the core challenge—the absence of a discrete vocabulary for image patches—is explicitly identified, and the proposed solution (masked image modeling using visual tokens from a DVAE) is logically motivated and well-illustrated. However, the most significant issue is a lack of critical depth in the positioning of the work relative to concurrent or closely related methods. The introduction acknowledges contrastive learning and self-distillation approaches but does not sufficiently differentiate BEIT from them in terms of conceptual limitations or failure cases. More pressingly, the paper references "pixel-level recovery" as a straightforward but flawed baseline without citing or discussing concurrent works that attempt exactly this approach (e.g., MAE or masked autoencoders), which were emerging around the same time. Without this contextualization, the novelty of the MIM task is somewhat overstated. Additionally, the claim of a "theoretical explanation from the perspective of variational autoencoder" is mentioned in the contributions but not elaborated in the provided excerpt, raising concerns about whether the method is truly framed as a VAE or merely loosely analogized. For a conference paper of this venue, a more rigorous and critical engagement with the landscape of contemporaneous masked image modeling approaches would strengthen the contribution and clarify the true technical novelty.

comments.title: Blockwise masking algorithm: aspect ratio range is degenerate
comments.explanation: The algorithm for blockwise masking draws the aspect ratio of the masking block from a uniform distribution over [0.3, 0.3], which always yields 0.3. This would make every masking block have the same shape (wider than tall), contradicting the intended variety and the typical implementation (e.g., in the original BEIT code, the aspect ratio is drawn from [0.3, 3.0]). The surrounding computations (a = sqrt(s·r), b = sqrt(s/r)) assume a meaningful aspect ratio. Given that the text was extracted via OCR, the second '0.3' is likely a misread of '3.0' or '3', but the error would prevent reproducibility if taken literally. Since the algorithm is a core contribution for pre-training, this is a moderate concern.

comments.title: Confusing explanation of VAE loss terms in Section 2.4
comments.quote: where (1) original image given input visual tokens; (3) qφ(z|x) denotes the image tokenizer that obtains visual tokens; (2) pθ(z|x̃) recovers the visual tokens based on the masked pψ(x|z) decodes the image, which is our MIM pre-training task.
comments.explanation: The sentence lists the three terms of the ELBO but gives them in an unnatural order (1, 3, 2) and contains a run-on phrase: 'pθ(z|x̃) recovers the visual tokens based on the masked pψ(x|z) decodes the image'. It appears the authors intend to say that pθ(z|x̃) recovers visual tokens (the MIM task) and pψ(x|z) decodes the image, but the punctuation and word order make it easy to misread. While the formal equation (2) is correctly given, the prose description could mislead a reader about which term corresponds to which component. This is a minor clarity issue, resolvable by rephrasing.

comments.title: Garbled notation for masked positions and corrupted image patches
comments.quote: The corrupted image patches x[M] = { x[p] i : i∈M}/N i=1 ∪ { e[M] : i ∈M}Ni=1 are then fed into the L-layer Transformer
comments.explanation: The definition of masked positions M is also garbled (appears as 'M ∈{ 1 , . . . , N }[0] .[4] N'), and the expression for constructing corrupted image patches is ambiguous with confusing indexing and union operation. While the intended meaning (mask a subset of patches and replace with learnable embedding) is clear from context and the prose, the formal notation is imprecise and could confuse readers trying to implement the method. This combines two related notational issues in the same paragraph.

comments.title: Underspecified computation of block dimensions a and b
comments.explanation: The formulas for 'a' and 'b' contain garbled square-root symbols. The intended computation is likely 'a = sqrt(s * r)' and 'b = sqrt(s / r)' (or rounding). However, the OCR corruption makes the exact operation unclear. A reader trying to implement Algorithm 1 would need to guess whether a and b are integers or floats, and how they interact with the later sampling bounds (t, l). This underspecification could lead to different implementations, affecting masking patterns and thus potentially experimental outcomes.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in masked position sampling bounds
comments.quote: t ← Rand(0, h − a) ; l ← Rand(0, w − b)
comments.explanation: The sampling bounds for t and l assume that a ≤ h and b ≤ w, but there is no explicit check or handling for cases where a > h or b > w. Since a and b are derived from s (which can be up to 0.4N = 0.4*h*w) and r, it's possible that a or b exceed the grid dimensions. The algorithm does not specify whether such cases are rejected, re-sampled, or handled via clipping. This omission could affect the reproducibility of the masking process.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in position embedding addition
comments.quote: The input vectors H0 = [e[S], Exp[i, ..., Ex]p[N] +] E[pos is fed] into Transformer.
comments.explanation: The notation suffers from OCR corruption and ambiguous parentheses. It seems to intend: H0 = [e[S], E x^p_1, ..., E x^p_N] + E_pos, i.e., add position embeddings to all token embeddings. However, the garbled text makes it unclear whether position embeddings are added to the [S] token as well. For reproducibility, a reader needs to know if the [S] token receives a position embedding. Standard ViT and BERT practice is ambiguous (some add, some don't). This should be specified clearly.

comments.title: Omission of visual token grid dimensions in notation
comments.quote: we tokenize the image x ∈ R [H×W×C] into z = [ z1, ..., zN ] ∈ V[h×w]
comments.explanation: The notation 'V[h×w]' is unusual and underspecified. The text says the visual tokens form a 14×14 grid, but the notation uses 'h' and 'w' without defining them. Earlier, N was defined as number of patches = HW/P^2, and in experiments the grid is 14×14. The notation 'V[h×w]' could be misread as the vocabulary set V raised to the power (h*w) or as a tensor. The intended meaning (a sequence of discrete tokens of length h*w) is clear from context, but the formal notation is sloppy.

comments.title: Garbled description of the second training stage
comments.quote: In the second stage, we learn the priorwith the most likely visual tokens _pθ_ while keeping _z_ ̂ _qi φ_ = arg max and _pψ_ fixed. _z qφ_ We simplify( _z|xi_ ). Then Equation (2) can be rewritten as: _qφ_ ( **z** _|xi_ ) to a one-point distribution
comments.explanation: The text is heavily corrupted by OCR, making the description of the second-stage training nearly unintelligible. The intended meaning appears to be: we compute the most likely visual token for each position via argmax over q_φ, then simplify q_φ to a one-point distribution. However, the sentence is broken ('priorwith', misplaced underscores, missing parentheses and function names). A careful reader could guess the intended content from context and from knowledge of VQ-VAE-style training, but the garbled prose does not clearly communicate the procedure and could lead to misunderstanding about what is being maximized and which parameters are fixed. Since this section presents a key theoretical connection (VAE interpretation), the lack of clarity is a moderate issue.

comments.title: Missing equation after 'Then Equation (2) can be rewritten as:'
comments.quote: Then Equation (2) can be rewritten as: where the second term is our BEIT pre-training objective.
comments.explanation: The passage states that Equation (2) can be rewritten, but the actual rewritten equation is not shown in the extracted text. The sentence immediately continues with 'where the second term is our BEIT pre-training objective.' without displaying the equation itself. This gap may be due to an OCR omission of a displayed equation. While the general idea can be inferred from the surrounding text and from the earlier ELBO decomposition, a reader cannot verify the exact form of the rewritten loss. This is a minor omission that would be resolved by seeing the original PDF.

comments.title: Corrupted initialization scaling factor
comments.quote: we rescale the output matrices (i.e., the last linear projection within each sub-layer) of the self-attention module and the feed-forward network by ~~_√_~~ 12 _l_[.]
comments.explanation: The multiplicative factor is rendered as garbled OCR: '~~_√_~~ 12 _l_[.]'. The intended expression is likely something like '1/√(2l)' or '1/√(12l)', but the exact scaling is unclear. Proper initialization is important for reproducing the pre-training stability results. Although a reader familiar with Transformer initialization might guess the intent (e.g., from the DeiT paper or from the common practice of scaling by 1/√(2l) for residual branches), the garbled text prevents a precise understanding of the method. This is a minor issue because the overall initialization strategy (small random values + scaling) is described, but the specific numerical factor is lost.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: As an academic reviewer, I would assess this paper as a high-quality and well-structured contribution that addresses a timely and important problem in self-supervised vision learning. The clarity is strong: the motivation for adapting BERT-style pre-training to vision is clearly articulated, the core challenge—the absence of a discrete vocabulary for image patches—is explicitly identified, and the proposed solution (masked image modeling using visual tokens from a DVAE) is logically motivated and well-illustrated. However, the most significant issue is a lack of critical depth in the positioning of the work relative to concurrent or closely related methods. The introduction acknowledges contrastive learning and self-distillation approaches but does not sufficiently differentiate BEIT from them in terms of conceptual limitations or failure cases. More pressingly, the paper references "pixel-level recovery" as a straightforward but flawed baseline without citing or discussing concurrent works that attempt exactly this approach (e.g., MAE or masked autoencoders), which were emerging around the same time. Without this contextualization, the novelty of the MIM task is somewhat overstated. Additionally, the claim of a "theoretical explanation from the perspective of variational autoencoder" is mentioned in the contributions but not elaborated in the provided excerpt, raising concerns about whether the method is truly framed as a VAE or merely loosely analogized. For a conference paper of this venue, a more rigorous and critical engagement with the landscape of contemporaneous masked image modeling approaches would strengthen the contribution and clarify the true technical novelty.

comments.title: Blockwise masking algorithm: aspect ratio range is degenerate
comments.explanation: The algorithm for blockwise masking draws the aspect ratio of the masking block from a uniform distribution over [0.3, 0.3], which always yields 0.3. This would make every masking block have the same shape (wider than tall), contradicting the intended variety and the typical implementation (e.g., in the original BEIT code, the aspect ratio is drawn from [0.3, 3.0]). The surrounding computations (a = sqrt(s·r), b = sqrt(s/r)) assume a meaningful aspect ratio. Given that the text was extracted via OCR, the second '0.3' is likely a misread of '3.0' or '3', but the error would prevent reproducibility if taken literally. Since the algorithm is a core contribution for pre-training, this is a moderate concern.

comments.title: Confusing explanation of VAE loss terms in Section 2.4
comments.quote: where (1) original image given input visual tokens; (3) qφ(z|x) denotes the image tokenizer that obtains visual tokens; (2) pθ(z|x̃) recovers the visual tokens based on the masked pψ(x|z) decodes the image, which is our MIM pre-training task.
comments.explanation: The sentence lists the three terms of the ELBO but gives them in an unnatural order (1, 3, 2) and contains a run-on phrase: 'pθ(z|x̃) recovers the visual tokens based on the masked pψ(x|z) decodes the image'. It appears the authors intend to say that pθ(z|x̃) recovers visual tokens (the MIM task) and pψ(x|z) decodes the image, but the punctuation and word order make it easy to misread. While the formal equation (2) is correctly given, the prose description could mislead a reader about which term corresponds to which component. This is a minor clarity issue, resolvable by rephrasing.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of masked positions set M
comments.quote: where the masked positions are denoted as M ∈{ 1 , . . . , N }[0] .[4] N
comments.explanation: The notation 'M ∈{ 1 , . . . , N }[0] .[4] N' is garbled, likely due to OCR corruption. From context (Algorithm 1 and the masking ratio of 40%), the intended meaning is that M is a subset of {1, ..., N} with cardinality approximately 0.4N. The garbled text could mislead a reader about what M represents. However, the definition of M in Algorithm 1 and the surrounding prose (e.g., 'randomly mask approximately 40% image patches') make the intended meaning clear. The issue is localized and resolvable from context.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in corrupted image patches definition
comments.quote: The corrupted image patches x[M] = { x[p] i : i∈M}/N i=1 ∪ { e[M] : i ∈M}Ni=1 are then fed into the L-layer Transformer
comments.explanation: The notation 'x[M] = { x[p] i : i∈M}/N i=1 ∪ { e[M] : i ∈M}Ni=1' is ambiguous and poorly formatted. It appears to be trying to say that the corrupted image patches are the original patches for non-masked positions and the learnable embedding for masked positions. However, the union operation and the indexing (e.g., '/N i=1') are confusing. Given the prose ('replace the masked patches with a learnable embedding') and Figure 1, the intended meaning is clear: the input sequence is constructed by taking all N patches (original or replaced), but the formal expression is not precise. This could confuse readers trying to implement the method.

comments.title: Algorithm 1: aspect ratio range appears degenerate
comments.quote: r ← Rand(0 . 3, 0 . 3)  ▷ Aspect ratio of block
comments.explanation: The call 'Rand(0 . 3, 0 . 3)' would always return 0.3 (assuming Rand returns a value in the closed interval). This suggests a typo: the lower and upper bounds are identical. In the original BEIT paper (ICLR 2022), this is corrected to 'Rand(0.3, 1/0.3)' or similar to allow a range of aspect ratios. The current text would make blockwise masking deterministic in aspect ratio, contradicting the claim that the aspect ratio is 'randomly chosen.' This is a likely OCR error (missing fraction bar or range), but it would be a real issue for reproducibility if taken literally.

comments.title: Underspecified computation of block dimensions a and b
comments.explanation: The formulas for 'a' and 'b' contain garbled square-root symbols. The intended computation is likely 'a = sqrt(s * r)' and 'b = sqrt(s / r)' (or rounding). However, the OCR corruption makes the exact operation unclear. A reader trying to implement Algorithm 1 would need to guess whether a and b are integers or floats, and how they interact with the later sampling bounds (t, l). This underspecification could lead to different implementations, affecting masking patterns and thus potentially experimental outcomes.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in masked position sampling bounds
comments.quote: t ← Rand(0, h − a) ; l ← Rand(0, w − b)
comments.explanation: The sampling bounds for t and l assume that a ≤ h and b ≤ w, but there is no explicit check or handling for cases where a > h or b > w. Since a and b are derived from s (which can be up to 0.4N = 0.4*h*w) and r, it's possible that a or b exceed the grid dimensions. The algorithm does not specify whether such cases are rejected, re-sampled, or handled via clipping. This omission could affect the reproducibility of the masking process.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in position embedding addition
comments.quote: The input vectors H0 = [e[S], Exp[i, ..., Ex]p[N] +] E[pos is fed] into Transformer.
comments.explanation: The notation suffers from OCR corruption and ambiguous parentheses. It seems to intend: H0 = [e[S], E x^p_1, ..., E x^p_N] + E_pos, i.e., add position embeddings to all token embeddings. However, the garbled text makes it unclear whether position embeddings are added to the [S] token as well. For reproducibility, a reader needs to know if the [S] token receives a position embedding. Standard ViT and BERT practice is ambiguous (some add, some don't). This should be specified clearly.

comments.title: Omission of visual token grid dimensions in notation
comments.quote: we tokenize the image x ∈ R [H×W×C] into z = [ z1, ..., zN ] ∈ V[h×w]
comments.explanation: The notation 'V[h×w]' is unusual and underspecified. The text says the visual tokens form a 14×14 grid, but the notation uses 'h' and 'w' without defining them. Earlier, N was defined as number of patches = HW/P^2, and in experiments the grid is 14×14. The notation 'V[h×w]' could be misread as the vocabulary set V raised to the power (h*w) or as a tensor. The intended meaning (a sequence of discrete tokens of length h*w) is clear from context, but the formal notation is sloppy.

comments.title: Garbled description of the second training stage
comments.quote: In the second stage, we learn the priorwith the most likely visual tokens _pθ_ while keeping _z_ ˆ _qi φ_ = arg max and _pψ_ fixed. _z qφ_ We simplify( _z|xi_ ). Then Equation (2) can be rewritten as: _qφ_ ( **z** _|xi_ ) to a one-point distribution
comments.explanation: The text is heavily corrupted by OCR, making the description of the second-stage training nearly unintelligible. The intended meaning appears to be: we compute the most likely visual token for each position via argmax over q_φ, then simplify q_φ to a one-point distribution. However, the sentence is broken ('priorwith', misplaced underscores, missing parentheses and function names). A careful reader could guess the intended content from context and from knowledge of VQ-VAE-style training, but the garbled prose does not clearly communicate the procedure and could lead to misunderstanding about what is being maximized and which parameters are fixed. Since this section presents a key theoretical connection (VAE interpretation), the lack of clarity is a moderate issue.

comments.title: Missing equation after 'Then Equation (2) can be rewritten as:'
comments.quote: Then Equation (2) can be rewritten as: where the second term is our BEIT pre-training objective.
comments.explanation: The passage states that Equation (2) can be rewritten, but the actual rewritten equation is not shown in the extracted text. The sentence immediately continues with 'where the second term is our BEIT pre-training objective.' without displaying the equation itself. This gap may be due to an OCR omission of a displayed equation. While the general idea can be inferred from the surrounding text and from the earlier ELBO decomposition, a reader cannot verify the exact form of the rewritten loss. This is a minor omission that would be resolved by seeing the original PDF.

comments.title: Corrupted initialization scaling factor
comments.quote: we rescale the output matrices (i.e., the last linear projection within each sub-layer) of the self-attention module and the feed-forward network by ~~_√_~~ 12 _l_[.]
comments.explanation: The multiplicative factor is rendered as garbled OCR: '~~_√_~~ 12 _l_[.]'. The intended expression is likely something like '1/√(2l)' or '1/√(12l)', but the exact scaling is unclear. Proper initialization is important for reproducing the pre-training stability results. Although a reader familiar with Transformer initialization might guess the intent (e.g., from the DeiT paper or from the common practice of scaling by 1/√(2l) for residual branches), the garbled text prevents a precise understanding of the method. This is a minor issue because the overall initialization strategy (small random values + scaling) is described, but the specific numerical factor is lost.
